evidence.text:
Leadership Through Lived Experience
Our public involvement is co-led by Linda Kirby and Terry Emery, who bring complementary
perspectives on hearing and cognition as co-applicants. Linda, a retired university library assistant
diagnosed with congenital hearing loss in mid-life, combines professional expertise in clear
communication with personal insight into late-diagnosis challenges. Terry, a former copywriter who
contributed to the James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership on hearing and dementia, brings his
perspective on how hearing loss intersects with cognitive concerns.
Supporting them is Fahima Begum, our PPIE lead with over a decade of experience facilitating inclusive
research. She brings professional expertise and personal experience of caring for people with cognitive
impairment and hearing loss. This PPIE team works closely with Hannah Semeraro, RNID Head of
Insight and Evidence and audiologist.
Building an Inclusive Partnership
We will proactively recruit a diverse PPIE panel. Our commitment to inclusion means:
• Flexible participation: Hybrid meetings, with options for in-person, video, or phone involvement.
• Accessible formats: Large print, Easy Read materia

TACT+ app is not a regulated medical device. See pp. 6–7, 15–18.
11. Liaising with RDNs.
We developed this bid with our local RDN, NOCLOR and their Primary Care Research Delivery team,
with whom we co-run ENHANCE where we have started recruitment in GP practices in London,
Yorkshire and Humber, and have more areas ready (NIHR203670). For TACT+ we have engaged RDNs
in the North West, North East, Midlands, East, South Coast, and Wales to support site identification and
recruitment. See p. 10.
12. Open Access costs
We have not included open access costs.
Additional clarifications from referees
- Carers: Most participants will not have carers, but where relevant, costs will be included in the
economic evaluation.
- Health utility: We selected HUI3 due to its sensitivity to changes in cognition and hearing loss,
supported by studies in MCI and audiology populations.
- Implementation: WS4 now explicitly evaluates feasibility, fidelity, and adoption using NPT and Proctor’s
Outcomes and includes a PhD and RA post to embed implementation capacity and generate an NHS-
ready toolkit. See pp. 22–23.

evidence.text: We request £3.42m in research costs to deliver a definitive evaluation of TACT+, a hybrid clinical-digital pathway for hearing and cognition. Costs have been benchmarked against NIHR guidance and structured around deliverables. All research (non-NHS) costs are included in this request. 1. Lead & Co-applicants (£994.4k) Fourteen co-applicants contribute clinical, digital, methodological and PPI multidisciplinary expertise across all workstreams. FTEs were increased from Stage 1 in response to panel concern over low time allocations. All costs follow institutional salary rates and match peak activity periods. An overall Programme Manager is necessary to manage and integration across work streams, disciplines and sites. CCTU co-applicants (statistical, health economic and governance leads) are also included here. Their roles are essential to ensure delivery pace, trial oversight, and rigour in a multi-site hybrid RCT. Their inclusion follows NIHR guidance on embedding experienced trialists in complex evaluations. 2. CCTU Delivery Staff (£930.5k) The UCL CCTU provides dedicated delivery support including: - 1.0 FTE Trial Manager - 0.5 FTE Clinical Project Manager - 0.5 FTE Statistician

- 0.3 FTE Health Economist - 0.3 FTE Data Scientist - 0.5 FTE Programme Administrator - 0.7 FTE Data Manager - 0.2 FTE Database Programmer - 0.3 FTE Site Set-up Manager These roles deliver trial set-up, data management, statistical analysis, adherence tracking, and regulatory reporting. CCTU’s involvement ensures MHRA, UK-GDPR and NIHR compliance and reflects UCL norms for hosted NIHR trials. These costs represent ~27% of the total and are proportionate to the trial’s scale, complexity, digital components, and multi-workstream structure of TACT+. 3. Research Delivery & Capacity-Building Staff (£645.0k) Includes: - 1.0 FTE Research Audiologist (M7–45) - 1.0 FTE Research Assistant (M4–45) - 1.0 FTE Research Associate (M22–63) - 2.0 FTE PhD fees and stipends (UCL & Nottingham) These posts support trial delivery (field testing, onboarding, follow-up) while building UK research capacity. All include protected time and formal training plans. The total meets the NIHR PGfAR target of 10–20% capacity spend (~11% net), and directly addresses national gaps in digital audiology, behavioural trials and dementia prevention. 4. Digital Infrastructure & Development Essential for delivering a digi

tally enabled NHS-ready model and ensuring reproducibility, adherence monitoring and coach communication. App Development & Hosting (£223.5k): Quote from Glitchers, trusted ENHANCE contractor. Includes specification, build, MVP, optimisation, and NHS login. Phased, fixed-cost stages ensure deliverability and open-source release. Includes Cloud services (£34k): Secure hosting, video, secure messaging and alerts (36 months). Database & Randomisation (£46.0k): OpenClinica SaaS and compliant eTMF system. GDPR compliant secure research data hosting, generation and transcription costs (11.8K) 5. SOECAT Research Costs (130.5k) Activities include time for personnel for recruitment and research data collection, EHR search, DocMail and SMS follow-up, hearing coach training, research data collection and data entry, and site coordination activities. SoECAT was validated jointly with SoECAT specialist. In addition to Research cost: NHS Service Support: £10.8K. Excess Treatment Costs: £88.6K. 5. Equipment & Consumables for intervention and trial delivery Tympa kits x18 (£68.6k): Combined otoscopy, audiometry and wax removal with included Tympa Academy training. Enables safe, rapid assessment by

evidence.text:
Research Question
Is "TACT+"—a proactive, primary care embedded hearing pathway combining handheld audiometry,
self-fitting hearing aids and remote coaching—effective and cost effective in slowing cognitive decline for
adults ≥55 with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and hearing loss versus usual care?
Background
MCI affects up to one in five older adults, with 14 to 23% progressing to dementia every 18 months. At
the same time, 25% of UK adults aged 50–89 have hearing loss—a key modifiable dementia risk.
Untreated hearing loss increases cognitive load, accelerates neurodegeneration and fuels social
isolation, yet fewer than 15% of those who could benefit use hearing aids. In our TACT pilot, run in
memory services, personalised support more than tripled regular aid use (22→75%). The ACHIEVE trial
reported a 62% reduction in cognitive decline among high-risk groups, comparable to our target
population.
We will adapt the intervention for Primary Care “hearing–cognition hubs” using handheld Tympa testing
and self-fitting hearing aids, supported by a coach and an app. The app enables goal-setting,
rehabilitation games, reminders and secure coach messaging, and coaches guide fitting, tro

Professor Sergi Costafreda Gonzalez

University College London

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Department:
Mental Health of Older People

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Proposed Role:
I led the TACT pilot that proved supported hearing aids work for people with cognitive impairment: 75% uptake versus 22% in standard audiological care. I am positioned to adapt this innovation for primary care delivery and test its cognitive impact at scale through TACT+. As Professor of Psychiatry of Ageing at UCL and consultant at Islington Memory Service, I bridge research and clinical reality. I've spent a decade treating MCI while building research partnerships with GP practices. I currently direct ENHANCE (£2.9m NIHR programme), successfully recruiting healthy older adults through primary care networks—demonstrating my ability to engage GPs and navigate primary care pathways directly relevant to TACT+. Through ENHANCE, I've learned to design interventions that work when delivered by non-specialists, create training that builds confidence in primary care teams, and develop digital tools that work for older people and support busy practices. My £8m research portfolio spans sensory health, dementia prevention, technology and implementation science, generating 27,000 citations and shaping international policy through the Lancet Dementia Commission. I will reduce clinical sessions from 3 to 2 weekly to provide hands-on leadership across all five workstreams. My co-investigators bring multidisciplinary expertise in hearing research, primary care, implementation and trial operational excellence. TACT+ represents the convergence of my clinical expertise in cognitive ageing, proven ability to deliver complex interventions through primary care, and vision for accessible dementia prevention. I have the experience, relationships, and team to make this work in the real NHS.

Dr Ruan-Ching Yu brings unique qualifications to lead TACT+ programme management. As first author on the TACT pilot trial publication (Yu et al., 2025) and of the systematic review establishing hearing-dementia links (Yu et al., 2024), and with a PhD on cultural adaptation and validation of cognitive testing for MCI, she possesses deep knowledge of both the intervention and its evidence base. Her coordination of TACT pilot and this complex NIHR application shows her organisational capability. She has assembled a multidisciplinary research team, worked with PPI, navigated NHS governance structures, engaged Primary Care and RDNs, and built enthusiasm across diverse stakeholders. This achievement reveals her ability to translate between clinical, academic, and operational worlds. While new to formal programme management, Dr Yu will be supported by UCL CCTU's dedicated trial coordinator and team, ensuring operational excellence. This structure allows her to focus on scientific leadership while developing PM expertise through mentored practice. As Programme Manager reporting to the CI, Dr Yu will transform strategic vision into executable plans. She will coordinate across five workstreams and multiple sites, managing day-to-day delivery and budget oversight. Her responsibilities span co-production workshops (WS1), field-testing logistics (WS2), trial operations partnership with CCTU (WS3), and implementation outputs (WS4-5). Dr Yu's scientific expertise in hearing-cognition pathways ensures clinical relevance drives operational decisions. Her proven ability to build consensus among diverse stakeholders provides the relationship skills essential for multi-site coordination.

Prof Anne Schilder is a Consultant ENT surgeon at UCLH and trialist, NIHR Senior Investigator and Director of the NIHR UCLH BRC Hearing Health Theme, where she uses qualitative and quantitative research methods to establish how best to educate ear and hearing care professionals about the role of cognition in hearing care for older adults, how to detect early cognitive impairment in ear and hearing clinics for older adults and provide tailored care to those identified with early cognitive impairment (Omar et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2025). As the past NIHR CRN ENT National Specialty Lead she has a large network across specialties involved in the proposed TACT+ programme and in- depth knowledge of research delivery within the NHS setting. She will provide senior clinical academic guidance throughout the programme and work with ENT and Audiology Specialties to ensure the trial intervention is robust and practically implementable within the NHS. Her experience in establishing best practice in hearing care will be vital in navigating the novel governance and clinical safety challenges of implementing a supported self-fitting pathway in a non- specialist primary care setting. She will oversee and actively participate in the co-production of the TACT+ pathway and its field testing (WS1, WS2). She will provide expert input in the definitive clinical trial protocol and advise on trial set- up and delivery (WS3), as well as the development of the implementation toolkit and dissemination strategy (WS4, WS5)

Ross Dunne is a Consultant Psychiatrist and Director of the Greater Manchester Dementia Research Centre. He led development of the "Mild Cognitive Impairment: the Manchester consensus" (Dunne et al., 2021), which has transformed clinical practice across the UK. This highly-cited publication provided the first UK expert consensus on diagnosing and managing MCI, standardising practice where formal guidance was lacking. Within TACT+, Dr Dunne will provide senior clinical oversight for cognitive eligibility criteria and lead the Manchester field-testing site. He will establish primary care partnerships and operational infrastructure for WS2 delivery, leveraging his local relationships to ensure efficient site set-up. His focus on equitable recruitment will help engage diverse Greater Manchester communities while ensuring trial governance meets NHS standards across mental health and primary care settings. He will support co-production of the TACT+ pathway, developing clinically grounded inclusion criteria (WS1), lead Manchester field- testing operations (WS2), co-develop the trial protocol for identifying potential MCI cases in primary care (WS3), and contribute to interpretation and dissemination of findings (WS5). His MCI expertise and established Manchester partnerships ensure TACT+ can rapidly mobilise in the region while reaching communities often underrepresented in research.

She leads a programme of research in auditory rehabilitation and cognitive training, with expertise in promoting hearing aid use. As Chair of the British Society of Audiology's Cognition in Hearing Special Interest Group, she bridges hearing and cognitive sciences. She recently led the NIHR James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership for Dementia and Hearing Conditions, bringing patients, carers, and clinicians together to identify the field's top research priorities. Dr Henshaw will apply her behavioural science expertise to design the TACT+ support package, ensuring it maximises engagement and adherence among people with cognitive impairment. She will lead development of the hearing coach manual and hearing rehabilitation components of the TACT+ app during co-production (WS1). She will co- lead the optimisation and field-testing phase (WS2) and lead evaluation of intervention components within the process evaluation (WS4). Her unique combination of cognitive psychology, hearing rehabilitation expertise, and patient priority insights ensures TACT+ interventions are both evidence-based and aligned with user needs.

As Director of a home care company in East London, she has extensive first-hand expertise supporting older adults with cognitive impairment and working with underserved communities across healthcare settings. This dual perspective gives her unique insight into the health inequalities and access barriers TACT+ aims to address. Ms Khatun will lead the programme's integrated PPIE and EDI strategy. She will ensure meaningful involvement is embedded throughout, grounded in established frameworks (UK Standards for Public Involvement, 4Pi). She will co-design and facilitate inclusive co-production workshops (WS1), develop the EDI strategy to support recruitment from underserved communities (WS3), co-chair the PPIE panel to shape research throughout (WS1-5), and ensure dissemination outputs are co-produced and accessible for people with hearing loss and cognitive difficulties (WS5). Her unique combination of academic PPIE leadership and daily experience caring for older adults with cognitive impairment ensures TACT+ remains grounded in real-world needs while advancing equity in dementia prevention research.

Dr Liz Steed is Reader in Health Psychology and an expert in behaviour change and implementation science. She has extensive experience developing theory-based interventions for successful trials (STAMINA, RP-PG-1016-2007) and leading process evaluations in implementation trials (IMP2ART RRP-PG-1016-20008, PETAL). As implementation lead for ENHANCE, she has established strong working relationships with the current team. Internationally, she teaches behaviour change in pulmonary rehabilitation and is an active member of the European Respiratory Society's Psychology and Behavioural Scientists division. Dr Steed will lead TACT+'s process evaluation and implementation strategy, ensuring the pathway is grounded in behavioural and implementation theory while generating deep understanding of how it can be adopted and sustained across diverse NHS primary care contexts. She will co-lead co-production using the COM-B model and Theoretical Domains Framework (WS1). Her leadership will be central to WS4, where she will conduct mixed-methods process evaluation using Normalisation Process Theory and develop the theory-informed implementation toolkit for NHS adoption. Her expertise ensures TACT+ moves beyond efficacy to real-world implementation from the outset.

As Head of Insight and Evidence at RNID, Dr Semeraro brings subject- matter expertise and connects TACT+ to the communities RNID supports. She will ensure the intervention is patient-centred, equitable, and informed by lived experience. RNID's extensive networks and insights into barriers faced by people with hearing loss will strengthen the design, delivery, and dissemination of TACT+. Dr Semeraro will support co-production of the TACT+ pathway and co- lead development of the training package for primary care staff, drawing on her audiology teaching experience (WS1). She will co-lead public and third-sector engagement activities, ensuring outputs are accessible and inclusive (WS5). RNID will publicise the trial through its communication channels, support recruitment via its networks, and help disseminate findings. RNID's policymaker relationships will support translation of TACT+ findings into national policy recommendations (WS5). Gareth Smith is Clinician Scientist in Audiology, Head of Audiological Science and Clinical Lead at Mid and South Essex NHS Foundation Trust. As former President of the British Academy of Audiology and current Audiology Editor for ENT & Audiology News, his expertise lies in strategic conception and operational delivery of advanced clinical audiology services within the NHS. In TACT+, Mr Smith will lead technical and clinical development of the hearing intervention. Crucially, he will guide selection of appropriate self-fitting hearing aids for our MCI population, drawing on his deep knowledge of the rapidly evolving OTC market and clinical evidence base. His frontline NHS experience ensures we choose devices that balance innovation with practical usability for cognitively vulnerable users. He will co-create protocols converting Tympa diagnostic data into safe fitting parameters and remote validation dashboards operable by primary care staff, ensuring the pathway is clinically sound and implementable within NHS realities. He will co-lead development and operationalisation of the pathway (WS1-2) and provide expert guidance on NHS device procurement and data tracking (WS4). His clinical acumen and finger on the pulse of hearing technology ensures TACT+ selects and implements solutions that work in real- world practice.

Leadership Through Lived Experience
Our public involvement is co-led by Linda Kirby and Terry Emery, who bring complementary
perspectives on hearing and cognition as co-applicants. Linda, a retired university library assistant
diagnosed with congenital hearing loss in mid-life, combines professional expertise in clear
communication with personal insight into late-diagnosis challenges. Terry, a former copywriter who
contributed to the James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership on hearing and dementia, brings his
perspective on how hearing loss intersects with cognitive concerns.

impairment and hearing loss. This PPIE team works closely with Hannah Semeraro, RNID Head of
Insight and Evidence and audiologist.

It comprehensively covers the problem (MCI/hearing loss), objectives, methods (RCT, self-fitting aids, coaching), beneficiaries, and impact (delaying dementia, reducing inequalities). evidence.text:
As we get older, some of us start to have trouble with memory or thinking clearly. When these changes
are noticeable but people can still manage daily life, doctors may call it mild cognitive impairment (MCI).
About one in six people over 60 have MCI, and about 15% of them develop dementia every 18 months.
Hearing loss is also common in later life. It can make everyday life harder — conversations become
difficult, people withdraw socially, and loneliness follows. Hearing loss can make memory worse. The
brain has to work harder to hear, and the parts that process sound shrink.
Hearing aids help people stay connected, feel better, and live more independently. They may also help
protect thinking and memory. But many people with memory problems don’t get hearing aids, or struggle
to use them.
In a small study with 58 people who had both MCI and hearing loss, we tested a new approach: doing
hearing tests and fitting hearing aids right away, with three months of support from a hearing coach. The
results were encouraging:
* 75% used their hearing aids regularly (compared to only 22% with usual care)
* People did better on memory tests and felt better, although a larger study is needed to
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
confirm this
We now plan a bigger study. To find people earlier, we will work in GP practices, where mild memory
difficulties often first appear.
Half the participants will get hearing aids they fit themselves, with help from a hearing coach. The coach
will help them get used to the aids, fix problems, and keep using them. People will also get an app with
reminders and a way to contact the coach. The goal is better hearing, mood, social life, and quality of
life. This may delay more serious memory problems like dementia.
The other half will receive usual care: a letter with their hearing test results and advice to speak to their
GP about hearing referral.
After 18 months, we will compare memory, hearing aid use, mood, social links, quality of life, and costs
between the two groups.
The whole project will take five years. The first 18 months will focus on setting up two hubs in
Manchester and London for early testing. Local practices and memory services will refer people there.
The next three and a half years will involve running the full trial and analysing the results.
Alongside the trial, we’ll also look at how this approach works in real life. We’ll try it out in NHS
Neighbourho

evidence.text:
od Health Centres and GP practices, and talk to patients and staff about their experiences.
What we learn will help us make it easier for the NHS to use this approach in the future.
People and Communities:
Our lived experience panel has shaped this project from the start and will continue to be involved. They
will co-design the intervention, help with study materials and recruitment and guide how we share results
and plan for future use in the NHS.
We are committed to including people from all backgrounds, especially those who are often left out of
research. Our team has a strong track record of working with diverse communities, so the findings can
be useful for everyone.
Sharing the Results:
We will share what we learn with the public, national charities, and patient groups — who will also help
us spread the word. Together, we’ll share updates through a website, social media, and newsletters,
and we’ll present at public events and publish in journals. If it works well, we’ll use what we’ve learned to
help the NHS offer this approach more widely.

The logic flows from the problem of untreated hearing loss accelerating neurodegeneration to the proposed solution.
criteria.drawbacks: The provided text is an abstract and summary, not a full literature review section. While it references key studies, a comprehensive critical analysis of the broader evidence base is not fully visible in the scoped text, limiting the score for depth of review.

evidence.text:
Research Question
Is "TACT+"—a proactive, primary care embedded hearing pathway combining handheld audiometry,
self-fitting hearing aids and remote coaching—effective and cost effective in slowing cognitive decline for
adults ≥55 with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and hearing loss versus usual care?
Background
MCI affects up to one in five older adults, with 14 to 23% progressing to dementia every 18 months. At
the same time, 25% of UK adults aged 50–89 have hearing loss—a key modifiable dementia risk.
Untreated hearing loss increases cognitive load, accelerates neurodegeneration and fuels social
isolation, yet fewer than 15% of those who could benefit use hearing aids. In our TACT pilot, run in
memory services, personalised support more than tripled regular aid use (22→75%). The ACHIEVE trial
reported a 62% reduction in cognitive decline among high-risk groups, comparable to our target
population.
We will adapt the intervention for Primary Care “hearing–cognition hubs” using handheld Tympa testing
and self-fitting hearing aids, supported by a coach and an app. The app enables goal-setting,
rehabilitation games, reminders and secure coach messaging, and coaches guide fitting, tro

1. Some intervention development and piloting already done - value for money?
Our Stage 2 request is £3.42M (£50K less than Stage 1) despite expanded scope and ambition. We
have shortened the overall duration from 72 to 66 months by shifting recruitment from memory services
to higher-throughput primary care hubs. Our previous pilot work showed high hearing aid use with
support, but further development is needed to scale the intervention to adapt it for mostly remote rather
than fully face-to-face support and for deployment in primary care.
2. Recruiting from memory clinics intervenes too late
We agree. The revised model now identifies and supports people with MCI earlier in primary care,
requiring three key changes:
- Intervention: We moved from audiologist-led fitting which would not be scalable in primary care, to a
supported self-fitting model leveraging technology and using CE/UKCA-marked OTC hearing aids and
Tympa for assessment, all delivered by trained non-specialists with remote audiology input which is
deliverable in primary care and aligned with direction of NHS policy.
- Primary Outcome: We replaced the ACE-III with the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) a briefer,

Feasibility is strongly supported by the shift to primary care hubs, use of CE-marked devices, and prior pilot success.

criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the scope is ambitious but well-justified by the team's track record and the detailed work plan.

Aims
Co-develop, evaluate and enable national roll out of TACT+ to preserve cognition, reduce inequalities
and improve quality of life for people with MCI and hearing loss.
Objectives
1. Co-design the TACT+ intervention, coaching, app and training (WS1).
2. Test feasibility in two PCN hubs (WS2).
3. Establish clinical and economic effectiveness in a large-scale RCT (WS3).
4. Conduct a mixed methods implementation study and NHS-ready toolkit (WS4).
5. Dissemination of results to influence policy, practice and public uptake, focusing on underserved
groups (WS5).
Methods
WS1 Co design (M1–12): co- workshops apply the Behaviour Change Wheel to co-create Tympa
assessment, immediate self-fit of hearing aids, app coaching and NHS audiology links.
WS2 Field Testing (M7–18): Two contrasting hubs (London, Manchester) enrol 62 participants to test
recruitment, workflows, feasibility and equity strategies.
WS3 RCT (M16–63): Two arm RCT with masked assessors (n = 424, 12 sites), including a 6-month
internal pilot. Eligibility: 55 y, MoCA 18–25 (screening rather than formal MCI diagnosis), symmetrical
30–60 dB loss. Intervention: TAC

T+; Care as usual: audiometry feedback with advice to consult GP.
Primary outcome: MoCA change at 18 months; secondary outcomes: cost-effectiveness, dementia
incidence, hearing outcomes, mood, social function.
WS4 Implementation (M1–63): Normalisation Process Theory guided evaluation with fidelity metrics and
>50 stakeholder interviews to identify barriers, enablers and core-plus-adaptable elements; outputs
include commissioning briefs, training resources and budget tools.
WS5 Dissemination (M36–63): Co created lay summaries, policy briefs, CPD resources and open-
source code; partnerships with RNID, NIHR Evidence and third sector groups maximise reach into
deprived and ethnic minority communities.
Impact
If effective, TACT+ will offer a low cost, digitally enabled route to preserve cognition, halve progression
to dementia, and reduce inequalities by reaching high deprivation PCNs. Outputs - open access toolkit,
commissioning brief, and co-developed PPIE resources—will position the NHS to roll out hearing care
prevention at scale within six years.

support, but further development is needed to scale the intervention to adapt it for mostly remote rather

Impact
If effective, TACT+ will offer a low cost, digitally enabled route to preserve cognition, halve progression

l-validated tool requiring less training and specialised staff and feasible for primary care delivery.
- Team: We increased FTEs for Drs Maru (primary care), Henshaw (behavioural science), and Steed
(implementation) to reflect expanded roles in design and delivery.
3. Why is a trial needed
We need an RCT to determine if supported hearing aid use results in meaningful cognitive benefit and
likely to lead to dementia prevention, as this is the likely 'game-changer' in terms of patient benefit and
costs that can create the drive to improve the current care as usual (CAU) model for hearing care that
fails most people with MCI. We also need a trial of TACT+ vs CAU to determine broader and important
patient benefits, on mood, social interaction and quality of life. Finally we need a trial to show if TACT+ is
cost-effective relative to CAU. Full rationale with references is in p4 of the Scientific case.
4. Primary Outcome Timing
Eighteen months balances detection of cognitive change and feasibility. MoCA is sensitive within this
timeframe in converters vs stable MCI. Hearing aid habit formation consolidates in 6–12 months so
benefits of sustained use on cognition and other outcomes expect
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Changes from Previous Stage

evidence.text:
ed within this window. Readouts at 6
months (resource use via CSRI) and 12 months (all primary and secondary outcomes) will reduce recall
bias and strengthen imputation if is required. See p.16.
5. Effect size Justification and Sample Size
MoCA has strong evidence of sensitivity and MCID in MCI populations. A 1.5-point effect is conservative
and meaningful, endorsed by our PPIE contributors: MoCA has lower burden, and interpretability on
likely impact on daily life. Despite switching outcomes, our power calculations remain valid and the
sample size (n=424) unchanged under conservative assumptions. See p.17.
6. Inclusion of under-served populations
We will recruit from high-deprivation areas (IMD 1–4) using GP searches, SMS, and opportunistic
routes. We provide 4G tablets and coaching to reduce digital exclusion. Materials will be co-produced
with lived experience contributors for cultural and digital accessibility. WS4 oversamples INCLUDE
groups and explores contextual factors. The main trial will analyse outcomes by IMD, ethnicity, and age.
See pp. 9, 16, 19, 22–25.
7. Role of PPIE
PPIE shaped this application and will shape every workstream. Focus groups informed design, outcome

evidence.text:
Research Question
Is "TACT+"—a proactive, primary care embedded hearing pathway combining handheld audiometry,
self-fitting hearing aids and remote coaching—effective and cost effective in slowing cognitive decline for
adults ≥55 with mild cognitive impairment (MCI) and hearing loss versus usual care?
Background
MCI affects up to one in five older adults, with 14 to 23% progressing to dementia every 18 months. At
the same time, 25% of UK adults aged 50–89 have hearing loss—a key modifiable dementia risk.
Untreated hearing loss increases cognitive load, accelerates neurodegeneration and fuels social
isolation, yet fewer than 15% of those who could benefit use hearing aids. In our TACT pilot, run in
memory services, personalised support more than tripled regular aid use (22→75%). The ACHIEVE trial
reported a 62% reduction in cognitive decline among high-risk groups, comparable to our target
population.
We will adapt the intervention for Primary Care “hearing–cognition hubs” using handheld Tympa testing
and self-fitting hearing aids, supported by a coach and an app. The app enables goal-setting,
rehabilitation games, reminders and secure coach messaging, and coaches guide fitting, tro

T+; Care as usual: audiometry feedback with advice to consult GP.
Primary outcome: MoCA change at 18 months; secondary outcomes: cost-effectiveness, dementia
incidence, hearing outcomes, mood, social function.
WS4 Implementation (M1–63): Normalisation Process Theory guided evaluation with fidelity metrics and
>50 stakeholder interviews to identify barriers, enablers and core-plus-adaptable elements; outputs
include commissioning briefs, training resources and budget tools.
WS5 Dissemination (M36–63): Co created lay summaries, policy briefs, CPD resources and open-
source code; partnerships with RNID, NIHR Evidence and third sector groups maximise reach into
deprived and ethnic minority communities.
Impact
If effective, TACT+ will offer a low cost, digitally enabled route to preserve cognition, halve progression
to dementia, and reduce inequalities by reaching high deprivation PCNs. Outputs - open access toolkit,
commissioning brief, and co-developed PPIE resources—will position the NHS to roll out hearing care
prevention at scale within six years.

As we get older, some of us start to have trouble with memory or thinking clearly. When these changes
are noticeable but people can still manage daily life, doctors may call it mild cognitive impairment (MCI).
About one in six people over 60 have MCI, and about 15% of them develop dementia every 18 months.
Hearing loss is also common in later life. It can make everyday life harder — conversations become
difficult, people withdraw socially, and loneliness follows. Hearing loss can make memory worse. The
brain has to work harder to hear, and the parts that process sound shrink.
Hearing aids help people stay connected, feel better, and live more independently. They may also help
protect thinking and memory. But many people with memory problems don’t get hearing aids, or struggle
to use them.
In a small study with 58 people who had both MCI and hearing loss, we tested a new approach: doing
hearing tests and fitting hearing aids right away, with three months of support from a hearing coach. The
results were encouraging:
* 75% used their hearing aids regularly (compared to only 22% with usual care)
* People did better on memory tests and felt better, although a larger study is needed to
evidence.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

evidence.text:
confirm this
We now plan a bigger study. To find people earlier, we will work in GP practices, where mild memory
difficulties often first appear.
Half the participants will get hearing aids they fit themselves, with help from a hearing coach. The coach
will help them get used to the aids, fix problems, and keep using them. People will also get an app with
reminders and a way to contact the coach. The goal is better hearing, mood, social life, and quality of
life. This may delay more serious memory problems like dementia.
The other half will receive usual care: a letter with their hearing test results and advice to speak to their
GP about hearing referral.
After 18 months, we will compare memory, hearing aid use, mood, social links, quality of life, and costs
between the two groups.
The whole project will take five years. The first 18 months will focus on setting up two hubs in
Manchester and London for early testing. Local practices and memory services will refer people there.
The next three and a half years will involve running the full trial and analysing the results.
Alongside the trial, we’ll also look at how this approach works in real life. We’ll try it out in NHS
Neighbourho

Aims
Co-develop, evaluate and enable national roll out of TACT+ to preserve cognition, reduce inequalities
and improve quality of life for people with MCI and hearing loss.
Objectives
1. Co-design the TACT+ intervention, coaching, app and training (WS1).
2. Test feasibility in two PCN hubs (WS2).
3. Establish clinical and economic effectiveness in a large-scale RCT (WS3).
4. Conduct a mixed methods implementation study and NHS-ready toolkit (WS4).
5. Dissemination of results to influence policy, practice and public uptake, focusing on underserved
groups (WS5).
Methods
WS1 Co design (M1–12): co- workshops apply the Behaviour Change Wheel to co-create Tympa
assessment, immediate self-fit of hearing aids, app coaching and NHS audiology links.
WS2 Field Testing (M7–18): Two contrasting hubs (London, Manchester) enrol 62 participants to test
recruitment, workflows, feasibility and equity strategies.
WS3 RCT (M16–63): Two arm RCT with masked assessors (n = 424, 12 sites), including a 6-month
internal pilot. Eligibility: 55 y, MoCA 18–25 (screening rather than formal MCI diagnosis), symmetrical
30–60 dB loss. Intervention: TAC

T+; Care as usual: audiometry feedback with advice to consult GP.
Primary outcome: MoCA change at 18 months; secondary outcomes: cost-effectiveness, dementia
incidence, hearing outcomes, mood, social function.
WS4 Implementation (M1–63): Normalisation Process Theory guided evaluation with fidelity metrics and
>50 stakeholder interviews to identify barriers, enablers and core-plus-adaptable elements; outputs
include commissioning briefs, training resources and budget tools.
WS5 Dissemination (M36–63): Co created lay summaries, policy briefs, CPD resources and open-
source code; partnerships with RNID, NIHR Evidence and third sector groups maximise reach into
deprived and ethnic minority communities.
Impact
If effective, TACT+ will offer a low cost, digitally enabled route to preserve cognition, halve progression
to dementia, and reduce inequalities by reaching high deprivation PCNs. Outputs - open access toolkit,
commissioning brief, and co-developed PPIE resources—will position the NHS to roll out hearing care
prevention at scale within six years.

od Health Centres and GP practices, and talk to patients and staff about their experiences.
What we learn will help us make it easier for the NHS to use this approach in the future.
People and Communities:
Our lived experience panel has shaped this project from the start and will continue to be involved. They
will co-design the intervention, help with study materials and recruitment and guide how we share results
and plan for future use in the NHS.
We are committed to including people from all backgrounds, especially those who are often left out of
research. Our team has a strong track record of working with diverse communities, so the findings can
be useful for everyone.
Sharing the Results:
We will share what we learn with the public, national charities, and patient groups — who will also help
us spread the word. Together, we’ll share updates through a website, social media, and newsletters,
and we’ll present at public events and publish in journals. If it works well, we’ll use what we’ve learned to
help the NHS offer this approach more widely.

evidence.text:
Leadership Through Lived Experience
Our public involvement is co-led by Linda Kirby and Terry Emery, who bring complementary
perspectives on hearing and cognition as co-applicants. Linda, a retired university library assistant
diagnosed with congenital hearing loss in mid-life, combines professional expertise in clear
communication with personal insight into late-diagnosis challenges. Terry, a former copywriter who
contributed to the James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership on hearing and dementia, brings his
perspective on how hearing loss intersects with cognitive concerns.
Supporting them is Fahima Begum, our PPIE lead with over a decade of experience facilitating inclusive
research. She brings professional expertise and personal experience of caring for people with cognitive
impairment and hearing loss. This PPIE team works closely with Hannah Semeraro, RNID Head of
Insight and Evidence and audiologist.
Building an Inclusive Partnership
We will proactively recruit a diverse PPIE panel. Our commitment to inclusion means:
• Flexible participation: Hybrid meetings, with options for in-person, video, or phone involvement.
• Accessible formats: Large print, Easy Read materia

evidence.text: We request £3.42m in research costs to deliver a definitive evaluation of TACT+, a hybrid clinical-digital pathway for hearing and cognition. Costs have been benchmarked against NIHR guidance and structured around deliverables. All research (non-NHS) costs are included in this request. 1. Lead & Co-applicants (£994.4k) Fourteen co-applicants contribute clinical, digital, methodological and PPI multidisciplinary expertise across all workstreams. FTEs were increased from Stage 1 in response to panel concern over low time allocations. All costs follow institutional salary rates and match peak activity periods. An overall Programme Manager is necessary to manage and integration across work streams, disciplines and sites. CCTU co-applicants (statistical, health economic and governance leads) are also included here. Their roles are essential to ensure delivery pace, trial oversight, and rigour in a multi-site hybrid RCT. Their inclusion follows NIHR guidance on embedding experienced trialists in complex evaluations. 2. CCTU Delivery Staff (£930.5k) The UCL CCTU provides dedicated delivery support including: - 1.0 FTE Trial Manager - 0.5 FTE Clinical Project Manager - 0.5 FTE Statistician

- 0.3 FTE Health Economist - 0.3 FTE Data Scientist - 0.5 FTE Programme Administrator - 0.7 FTE Data Manager - 0.2 FTE Database Programmer - 0.3 FTE Site Set-up Manager These roles deliver trial set-up, data management, statistical analysis, adherence tracking, and regulatory reporting. CCTU’s involvement ensures MHRA, UK-GDPR and NIHR compliance and reflects UCL norms for hosted NIHR trials. These costs represent ~27% of the total and are proportionate to the trial’s scale, complexity, digital components, and multi-workstream structure of TACT+. 3. Research Delivery & Capacity-Building Staff (£645.0k) Includes: - 1.0 FTE Research Audiologist (M7–45) - 1.0 FTE Research Assistant (M4–45) - 1.0 FTE Research Associate (M22–63) - 2.0 FTE PhD fees and stipends (UCL & Nottingham) These posts support trial delivery (field testing, onboarding, follow-up) while building UK research capacity. All include protected time and formal training plans. The total meets the NIHR PGfAR target of 10–20% capacity spend (~11% net), and directly addresses national gaps in digital audiology, behavioural trials and dementia prevention. 4. Digital Infrastructure & Development Essential for delivering a digi

tally enabled NHS-ready model and ensuring reproducibility, adherence monitoring and coach communication. App Development & Hosting (£223.5k): Quote from Glitchers, trusted ENHANCE contractor. Includes specification, build, MVP, optimisation, and NHS login. Phased, fixed-cost stages ensure deliverability and open-source release. Includes Cloud services (£34k): Secure hosting, video, secure messaging and alerts (36 months). Database & Randomisation (£46.0k): OpenClinica SaaS and compliant eTMF system. GDPR compliant secure research data hosting, generation and transcription costs (11.8K) 5. SOECAT Research Costs (130.5k) Activities include time for personnel for recruitment and research data collection, EHR search, DocMail and SMS follow-up, hearing coach training, research data collection and data entry, and site coordination activities. SoECAT was validated jointly with SoECAT specialist. In addition to Research cost: NHS Service Support: £10.8K. Excess Treatment Costs: £88.6K. 5. Equipment & Consumables for intervention and trial delivery Tympa kits x18 (£68.6k): Combined otoscopy, audiometry and wax removal with included Tympa Academy training. Professor Sergi Costafreda Gonzalez

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Organisation:
University College London

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Department:
Mental Health of Older People

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Proposed Role:
I led the TACT pilot that proved supported hearing aids work for people with cognitive impairment: 75% uptake versus 22% in standard audiological care. I am positioned to adapt this innovation for primary care delivery and test its cognitive impact at scale through TACT+. As Professor of Psychiatry of Ageing at UCL and consultant at Islington Memory Service, I bridge research and clinical reality. I've spent a decade treating MCI while building research partnerships with GP practices. I currently direct ENHANCE (£2.9m NIHR programme), successfully recruiting healthy older adults through primary care networks—demonstrating my ability to engage GPs and navigate primary care pathways directly relevant to TACT+. Through ENHANCE, I've learned to design interventions that work when delivered by non-specialists, create training that builds confidence in primary care teams, and develop digital tools that work for older people and support busy practices. My £8m research portfolio spans sensory health, dementia prevention, technology and implementation science, generating 27,000 citations and shaping international policy through the Lancet Dementia Commission. I will reduce clinical sessions from 3 to 2 weekly to provide hands-on leadership across all five workstreams. My co-investigators bring multidisciplinary expertise in hearing research, primary care, implementation and trial operational excellence. TACT+ represents the convergence of my clinical expertise in cognitive ageing, proven ability to deliver complex interventions through primary care, and vision for accessible dementia prevention. I have the experience, relationships, and team to make this work in the real NHS.

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [0] > Proposed Role:
Dr Ruan-Ching Yu brings unique qualifications to lead TACT+ programme management. As first author on the TACT pilot trial publication (Yu et al., 2025) and of the systematic review establishing hearing-dementia links (Yu et al., 2024), and with a PhD on cultural adaptation and validation of cognitive testing for MCI, she possesses deep knowledge of both the intervention and its evidence base. Her coordination of TACT pilot and this complex NIHR application shows her organisational capability. She has assembled a multidisciplinary research team, worked with PPI, navigated NHS governance structures, engaged Primary Care and RDNs, and built enthusiasm across diverse stakeholders. This achievement reveals her ability to translate between clinical, academic, and operational worlds. While new to formal programme management, Dr Yu will be supported by UCL CCTU's dedicated trial coordinator and team, ensuring operational excellence. This structure allows her to focus on scientific leadership while developing PM expertise through mentored practice. As Programme Manager reporting to the CI, Dr Yu will transform strategic vision into executable plans. She will coordinate across five workstreams and multiple sites, managing day-to-day delivery and budget oversight. Her responsibilities span co-production workshops (WS1), field-testing logistics (WS2), trial operations partnership with CCTU (WS3), and implementation outputs (WS4-5). Dr Yu's scientific expertise in hearing-cognition pathways ensures clinical relevance drives operational decisions. Her proven ability to build consensus among diverse stakeholders provides the relationship skills essential for multi-site coordination.

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [1] > Proposed Role:
Prof Anne Schilder is a Consultant ENT surgeon at UCLH and trialist, NIHR Senior Investigator and Director of the NIHR UCLH BRC Hearing Health Theme, where she uses qualitative and quantitative research methods to establish how best to educate ear and hearing care professionals about the role of cognition in hearing care for older adults, how to detect early cognitive impairment in ear and hearing clinics for older adults and provide tailored care to those identified with early cognitive impairment (Omar et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2025). As the past NIHR CRN ENT National Specialty Lead she has a large network across specialties involved in the proposed TACT+ programme and in- depth knowledge of research delivery within the NHS setting. She will provide senior clinical academic guidance throughout the programme and work with ENT and Audiology Specialties to ensure the trial intervention is robust and practically implementable within the NHS. Her experience in establishing best practice in hearing care will be vital in navigating the novel governance and clinical safety challenges of implementing a supported self-fitting pathway in a non- specialist primary care setting. She will oversee and actively participate in the co-production of the TACT+ pathway and its field testing (WS1, WS2). She will provide expert input in the definitive clinical trial protocol and advise on trial set- up and delivery (WS3), as well as the development of the implementation toolkit and dissemination strategy (WS4, WS5)

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [2] > Proposed Role:
Ross Dunne is a Consultant Psychiatrist and Director of the Greater Manchester Dementia Research Centre. He led development of the "Mild Cognitive Impairment: the Manchester consensus" (Dunne et al., 2021), which has transformed clinical practice across the UK. This highly-cited publication provided the first UK expert consensus on diagnosing and managing MCI, standardising practice where formal guidance was lacking. Within TACT+, Dr Dunne will provide senior clinical oversight for cognitive eligibility criteria and lead the Manchester field-testing site. He will establish primary care partnerships and operational infrastructure for WS2 delivery, leveraging his local relationships to ensure efficient site set-up. His focus on equitable recruitment will help engage diverse Greater Manchester communities while ensuring trial governance meets NHS standards across mental health and primary care settings. He will support co-production of the TACT+ pathway, developing clinically grounded inclusion criteria (WS1), lead Manchester field- testing operations (WS2), co-develop the trial protocol for identifying potential MCI cases in primary care (WS3), and contribute to interpretation and dissemination of findings (WS5). His MCI expertise and established Manchester partnerships ensure TACT+ can rapidly mobilise in the region while reaching communities often underrepresented in research.

Dr Helen Henshaw is a Cognitive Psychologist and Nottingham BRC Principal Research Fellow. She leads a programme of research in auditory rehabilitation and cognitive training, with expertise in promoting hearing aid use. As Chair of the British Society of Audiology's Cognition in Hearing Special Interest Group, she bridges hearing and cognitive sciences. She recently led the NIHR James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership for Dementia and Hearing Conditions, bringing patients, carers, and clinicians together to identify the field's top research priorities. Dr Henshaw will apply her behavioural science expertise to design the TACT+ support package, ensuring it maximises engagement and adherence among people with cognitive impairment. criteria.name: Inclusive Research Design
criteria.definition: Consideration of inclusivity throughout the research lifecycle, including participant diversity and accessibility.

signals.signal_text: Explicit strategies for diverse recruitment
Adaptive methods or accessibility considerations
Equity and participation indicators

criteria.pros: Inclusivity is a core design feature: recruitment targets high-deprivation areas (IMD 1-4) and ethnic minorities. Adaptive methods include 4G tablets for digital exclusion, Easy Read materials, and live captioning. Equity indicators are built into the analysis plan (stratification by IMD, ethnicity, age).
criteria.drawbacks: No drawbacks; the inclusive design is comprehensive, costed, and integrated throughout the research lifecycle.
criteria.rationale: No drawbacks; the inclusive design is comprehensive, costed, and integrated throughout the research lifecycle.

evidence.text:
T+; Care as usual: audiometry feedback with advice to consult GP.
Primary outcome: MoCA change at 18 months; secondary outcomes: cost-effectiveness, dementia
incidence, hearing outcomes, mood, social function.
WS4 Implementation (M1–63): Normalisation Process Theory guided evaluation with fidelity metrics and
>50 stakeholder interviews to identify barriers, enablers and core-plus-adaptable elements; outputs
include commissioning briefs, training resources and budget tools.
WS5 Dissemination (M36–63): Co created lay summaries, policy briefs, CPD resources and open-
source code; partnerships with RNID, NIHR Evidence and third sector groups maximise reach into
deprived and ethnic minority communities.
Impact
If effective, TACT+ will offer a low cost, digitally enabled route to preserve cognition, halve progression
to dementia, and reduce inequalities by reaching high deprivation PCNs. Outputs - open access toolkit,
commissioning brief, and co-developed PPIE resources—will position the NHS to roll out hearing care
prevention at scale within six years.

8. Plain English summary was excellent
Thank you, we have updated it.
9. Capacity building
We removed the MSc post and now support two PhDs (behaviour change, audiology) and two pre-
doctoral RAs (audiology, implementation). Trial staff, including the research audiologist, will receive
protected time, mentorship, and training. This aligns with PGfAR and NIHR Academy guidance and
represents 19% of the budget. Prof Livingston is the Named Training Contact. See pp. 26–27.
10. Timeline and regulatory risks
We revised the timeline for realism, in consultation with our CTU: reduced total duration, but lengthened
the preparatory phase for hiring and approvals. We do not anticipate needing MHRA approval as all
devices used will hold CE/UKCA marks

evidence.text:
Leadership Through Lived Experience
Our public involvement is co-led by Linda Kirby and Terry Emery, who bring complementary
perspectives on hearing and cognition as co-applicants. Linda, a retired university library assistant
diagnosed with congenital hearing loss in mid-life, combines professional expertise in clear
communication with personal insight into late-diagnosis challenges. Terry, a former copywriter who
contributed to the James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership on hearing and dementia, brings his
perspective on how hearing loss intersects with cognitive concerns.
Supporting them is Fahima Begum, our PPIE lead with over a decade of experience facilitating inclusive
research. She brings professional expertise and personal experience of caring for people with cognitive
impairment and hearing loss. This PPIE team works closely with Hannah Semeraro, RNID Head of
Insight and Evidence and audiologist.
Building an Inclusive Partnership
We will proactively recruit a diverse PPIE panel. Our commitment to inclusion means:
• Flexible participation: Hybrid meetings, with options for in-person, video, or phone involvement.
• Accessible formats: Large print, Easy Read materia

trained coaches in community hubs. The figure reflects prudent forward planning while remaining cost-conscious. sub_criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
sub_criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly outlines support for two PhDs (behaviour change, audiology) and two pre-doctoral RAs, with trial staff receiving protected time, mentorship, and training aligned to PGfAR and NIHR guidance. Specific training activities are identified, including 'Tympa Academy training' for coaches and formal training plans for research staff. The training topics (audiology, behaviour change, implementation) are directly relevant to the project's hybrid clinical-digital pathway.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the existence of training is clear, the application lacks a detailed structured timeline or sequencing of specific training activities (e.g., when specific workshops or courses will occur relative to project milestones). The description is high-level ('formal training plans') rather than a granular curriculum.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the existence of training is clear, the application lacks a detailed structured timeline or sequencing of specific training activities (e.g., when specific workshops or courses will occur relative to project milestones). The description is high-level ('formal training plans') rather than a granular curriculum.

sub_criteria.definition: How well the proposed training addresses the applicant’s current skills gaps and supports successful project delivery.

signals.signal_text: Identification of personal skill gaps
Clear mapping between training activities and project tasks
Evidence that proposed training is targeted rather than generic
Mention of mentorship, workshops, or formal courses relevant to development needs

sub_criteria.pros: The training is highly targeted to project needs, specifically addressing the shift to a 'supported self-fitting model' and 'remote audiology input' by training non-specialists. The application explicitly links training in behaviour change and audiology to the specific roles of the PhDs and RAs. Mentorship is provided by a Named Training Contact (Prof Livingston) and experienced trialists, ensuring the training is not generic but tailored to the complex multi-site RCT context.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The application does not explicitly articulate the specific 'skill gaps' of the individual trainees prior to the project; it assumes the training is needed based on the project's complexity rather than a personal skills audit. However, the alignment with project tasks is strong.
sub_criteria.rationale: The application does not explicitly articulate the specific 'skill gaps' of the individual trainees prior to the project; it assumes the training is needed based on the project's complexity rather than a personal skills audit. However, the alignment with project tasks is strong.

criteria.name: Quality and Relevance of Proposed Training
criteria.definition: How well-structured, clear, and relevant the training plan is in relation to the project’s goals and the applicant’s research needs.

signals.signal_text: Completeness and clarity of training description
Alignment of training topics with project methods or techniques
Structured timeline or sequencing of training activities
Explicit links between training content and skill acquisition

criteria.pros: The application explicitly outlines support for two PhDs (behaviour change, audiology) and two pre-doctoral RAs, with trial staff receiving protected time, mentorship, and training aligned to PGfAR and NIHR guidance. Specific training activities are identified, including 'Tympa Academy training' for coaches and formal training plans for research staff. The training topics (audiology, behaviour change, implementation) are directly relevant to the project's hybrid clinical-digital pathway.
criteria.drawbacks: While the existence of training is clear, the application lacks a detailed structured timeline or sequencing of specific training activities (e.g., when specific workshops or courses will occur relative to project milestones). The description is high-level ('formal training plans') rather than a granular curriculum.
criteria.rationale: While the existence of training is clear, the application lacks a detailed structured timeline or sequencing of specific training activities (e.g., when specific workshops or courses will occur relative to project milestones). The description is high-level ('formal training plans') rather than a granular curriculum.

sub_criteria.name: Track Record of the Contracting Organisation (and Partners)
sub_criteria.definition: Demonstrated expertise, experience, and research achievements of the organisation(s) in the proposed research area.

signals.signal_text: Publication or project history in relevant research fields
Previous funding success or awards in similar domains
Mention of prior collaborations or partnerships
Highlighted achievements or notable outputs

sub_criteria.pros: The application provides robust evidence of track record. The contracting organisation (UCL) and partners (NHS Trusts, RNID) demonstrate extensive expertise in hearing and dementia research. Specific achievements include the successful TACT pilot (75% uptake), the ENHANCE programme (£2.9m NIHR), and the Lancet Dementia Commission. The team includes NIHR Senior Investigators and leads of national initiatives, with a proven history of multi-site recruitment in primary care.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all signals regarding publication history, funding success, collaborations, and notable outputs.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the evidence is comprehensive and directly addresses all signals regarding publication history, funding success, collaborations, and notable outputs.

evidence.text:
and be deployed within intended use. Tympa is CE-marked.
TACT+ app is not a regulated medical device. See pp. 6–7, 15–18.
11. Liaising with RDNs.
We developed this bid with our local RDN, NOCLOR and their Primary Care Research Delivery team,
with whom we co-run ENHANCE where we have started recruitment in GP practices in London,
Yorkshire and Humber, and have more areas ready (NIHR203670). For TACT+ we have engaged RDNs
in the North West, North East, Midlands, East, South Coast, and Wales to support site identification and
recruitment. See p. 10.
12. Open Access costs
We have not included open access costs.
Additional clarifications from referees
- Carers: Most participants will not have carers, but where relevant, costs will be included in the
economic evaluation.
- Health utility: We selected HUI3 due to its sensitivity to changes in cognition and hearing loss,
supported by studies in MCI and audiology populations.
- Implementation: WS4 now explicitly evaluates feasibility, fidelity, and adoption using NPT and Proctor’s
Outcomes and includes a PhD and RA post to embed implementation capacity and generate an NHS-
ready toolkit. See pp. 22–23.

evidence.text:
SUMMARY INFORMATION > Application Title:
Treating auditory impairment to preserve cognition in people with mild cognitive impairment

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Contracting Organisation:
NORTH LONDON NHS FOUNDATION TRUST

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Department:
Services for Ageing and Mental Health

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Start Date:
01/04/2026

SUMMARY INFORMATION > End Date:
30/09/2031

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Duration (months):
66

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Total Cost to NIHR:
£3,420,354.46

SUMMARY INFORMATION > NHS Support Costs:
£10,822.98

SUMMARY INFORMATION > NHS Excess Treatment Costs:
£88,594.08

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Non-NHS Excess Treatment Costs:
£0.00

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Has this application been previously submitted to this or any other funding body?:
No

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Full Name:
Professor Sergi Costafreda Gonzalez

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Organisation:
University College London

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Department:
Mental Health of Older People

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Proposed Role:
I led the TACT pilot that proved supported hearing aids work for people with cognitive impairment: 75% uptake versus 22% in standard audiological care. I am positioned to adapt this innovation for primary care delivery and test its cognitive impact at scale through TACT+. As Professor of Psychiatry of Ageing at UCL and consultant at Islington Memory Service, I bridge research and clinical reality. I've spent a decade treating MCI while building research partnerships with GP practices. I currently direct ENHANCE (£2.9m NIHR programme), successfully recruiting healthy older adults through primary care networks—demonstrating my ability to engage GPs and navigate primary care pathways directly relevant to TACT+. Through ENHANCE, I've learned to design interventions that work when delivered by non-specialists, create training that builds confidence in primary care teams, and develop digital tools that work for older people and support busy practices. My £8m research portfolio spans sensory health, dementia prevention, technology and implementation science, generating 27,000 citations and shaping international policy through the Lancet Dementia Commission. I will reduce clinical sessions from 3 to 2 weekly to provide hands-on leadership across all five workstreams. My co-investigators bring multidisciplinary expertise in hearing research, primary care, implementation and trial operational excellence. TACT+ represents the convergence of my clinical expertise in cognitive ageing, proven ability to deliver complex interventions through primary care, and vision for accessible dementia prevention. I have the experience, relationships, and team to make this work in the real NHS.

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > ORCID:
0000-0001-6914-086X

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > % FTE Commitment:
17%

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [0] > Full Name:
Dr Ruan-Ching Yu

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [0] > Organisation:
University College London

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [0] > Department:
Mental Health of Older People

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [0] > Proposed Role:
Dr Ruan-Ching Yu brings unique qualifications to lead TACT+ programme management. As first author on the TACT pilot trial publication (Yu et al., 2025) and of the systematic review establishing hearing-dementia links (Yu et al., 2024), and with a PhD on cultural adaptation and validation of cognitive testing for MCI, she possesses deep knowledge of both the intervention and its evidence base. Her coordination of TACT pilot and this complex NIHR application shows her organisational capability. She has assembled a multidisciplinary research team, worked with PPI, navigated NHS governance structures, engaged Primary Care and RDNs, and built enthusiasm across diverse stakeholders. This achievement reveals her ability to translate between clinical, academic, and operational worlds. While new to formal programme management, Dr Yu will be supported by UCL CCTU's dedicated trial coordinator and team, ensuring operational excellence. This structure allows her to focus on scientific leadership while developing PM expertise through mentored practice. As Programme Manager reporting to the CI, Dr Yu will transform strategic vision into executable plans. She will coordinate across five workstreams and multiple sites, managing day-to-day delivery and budget oversight. Her responsibilities span co-production workshops (WS1), field-testing logistics (WS2), trial operations partnership with CCTU (WS3), and implementation outputs (WS4-5). Dr Yu's scientific expertise in hearing-cognition pathways ensures clinical relevance drives operational decisions. Her proven ability to build consensus among diverse stakeholders provides the relationship skills essential for multi-site coordination.

She will provide senior clinical academic guidance throughout the programme and work with ENT and Audiology Specialties to ensure the trial intervention is robust and practically implementable within the NHS. Her experience in establishing best practice in hearing care will be vital in navigating the novel governance and clinical safety challenges of implementing a supported self-fitting pathway in a non- specialist primary care setting. She will oversee and actively participate in the co-production of the TACT+ pathway and its field testing (WS1, WS2). This highly-cited publication provided the first UK expert consensus on diagnosing and managing MCI, standardising practice where formal guidance was lacking. Within TACT+, Dr Dunne will provide senior clinical oversight for cognitive eligibility criteria and lead the Manchester field-testing site. He will establish primary care partnerships and operational infrastructure for WS2 delivery, leveraging his local relationships to ensure efficient site set-up. His focus on equitable recruitment will help engage diverse Greater Manchester communities while ensuring trial governance meets NHS standards across mental health and primary care settings. He will support co-production of the TACT+ pathway, developing clinically grounded inclusion criteria (WS1), lead Manchester field- testing operations (WS2), co-develop the trial protocol for identifying potential MCI cases in primary care (WS3), and contribute to interpretation and dissemination of findings (WS5). His MCI expertise and established Manchester partnerships ensure TACT+ can rapidly mobilise in the region while reaching communities often underrepresented in research.

She has extensive experience developing theory-based interventions for successful trials (STAMINA, RP-PG-1016-2007) and leading process evaluations in implementation trials (IMP2ART RRP-PG-1016-20008, PETAL). As implementation lead for ENHANCE, she has established strong working relationships with the current team. Internationally, she teaches behaviour change in pulmonary rehabilitation and is an active member of the European Respiratory Society's Psychology and Behavioural Scientists division. Dr Steed will lead TACT+'s process evaluation and implementation strategy, ensuring the pathway is grounded in behavioural and implementation theory while generating deep understanding of how it can be adopted and sustained across diverse NHS primary care contexts. She will co-lead co-production using the COM-B model and Theoretical Domains Framework (WS1). Her leadership will be central to WS4, where she will conduct mixed-methods process evaluation using Normalisation Process Theory and develop the theory-informed implementation toolkit for NHS adoption. In WS3, she will advise on clinician and patient engagement strategies for inclusive recruitment across diverse communities. In WS4, she will co-lead development of the implementation toolkit and CPD resources, ensuring the hearing- coach model is ready for scalable primary care adoption. sub_criteria.name: Suitability of Supervisory Support and Mentorship Arrangements
sub_criteria.definition: Quality, clarity, and appropriateness of supervision and mentoring for supporting the applicant’s research and career development.

signals.signal_text: Explicit description of supervisor roles and responsibilities
Evidence of mentorship plans, meetings, or training support
Alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs
Presence of structured supervision framework (frequency, format, accountability)

sub_criteria.pros: Strong alignment between supervisor expertise and project needs is evident, with a multidisciplinary team covering audiology, primary care, and implementation science. Prof Livingston is explicitly named as the Training Contact, and the application details mentorship for PhDs and RAs. The team structure includes senior operational oversight (UCL CCTU) and clinical guidance from consultants.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While the roles and expertise are well-defined, the specific frequency or format of supervision meetings (e.g., weekly, monthly) is not explicitly detailed in the provided text, preventing a perfect score for the structured framework signal.
sub_criteria.rationale: While the roles and expertise are well-defined, the specific frequency or format of supervision meetings (e.g., weekly, monthly) is not explicitly detailed in the provided text, preventing a perfect score for the structured framework signal.

evidence.text:
selection, how to sensitively communicate assessment results to participants, benefits of hearing aids
beyond cognition, how to approach coaching, and app simplification. Two public contributors became
co-applicants and will continue to guide design, delivery, and governance. A PPIE co-investigator leads
involvement; a co-production group will support implementation and materials. Support and evaluation
structures are in place. See pp. 6–7, 24–25.
8. Plain English summary was excellent
Thank you, we have updated it.
9. Capacity building
We removed the MSc post and now support two PhDs (behaviour change, audiology) and two pre-
doctoral RAs (audiology, implementation). Trial staff, including the research audiologist, will receive
protected time, mentorship, and training. This aligns with PGfAR and NIHR Academy guidance and
represents 19% of the budget. Prof Livingston is the Named Training Contact. See pp. 26–27.
10. Timeline and regulatory risks
We revised the timeline for realism, in consultation with our CTU: reduced total duration, but lengthened
the preparatory phase for hiring and approvals. We do not anticipate needing MHRA approval as all
devices used will hold CE/UKCA marks

Professor Sergi Costafreda Gonzalez

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Organisation:
University College London

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Department:
Mental Health of Older People

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Proposed Role:
I led the TACT pilot that proved supported hearing aids work for people with cognitive impairment: 75% uptake versus 22% in standard audiological care. I am positioned to adapt this innovation for primary care delivery and test its cognitive impact at scale through TACT+. As Professor of Psychiatry of Ageing at UCL and consultant at Islington Memory Service, I bridge research and clinical reality. I've spent a decade treating MCI while building research partnerships with GP practices. I currently direct ENHANCE (£2.9m NIHR programme), successfully recruiting healthy older adults through primary care networks—demonstrating my ability to engage GPs and navigate primary care pathways directly relevant to TACT+. Through ENHANCE, I've learned to design interventions that work when delivered by non-specialists, create training that builds confidence in primary care teams, and develop digital tools that work for older people and support busy practices. My £8m research portfolio spans sensory health, dementia prevention, technology and implementation science, generating 27,000 citations and shaping international policy through the Lancet Dementia Commission. I will reduce clinical sessions from 3 to 2 weekly to provide hands-on leadership across all five workstreams. My co-investigators bring multidisciplinary expertise in hearing research, primary care, implementation and trial operational excellence. TACT+ represents the convergence of my clinical expertise in cognitive ageing, proven ability to deliver complex interventions through primary care, and vision for accessible dementia prevention. I have the experience, relationships, and team to make this work in the real NHS.

Dr Ruan-Ching Yu brings unique qualifications to lead TACT+ programme management. As first author on the TACT pilot trial publication (Yu et al., 2025) and of the systematic review establishing hearing-dementia links (Yu et al., 2024), and with a PhD on cultural adaptation and validation of cognitive testing for MCI, she possesses deep knowledge of both the intervention and its evidence base. Her coordination of TACT pilot and this complex NIHR application shows her organisational capability. She has assembled a multidisciplinary research team, worked with PPI, navigated NHS governance structures, engaged Primary Care and RDNs, and built enthusiasm across diverse stakeholders. This achievement reveals her ability to translate between clinical, academic, and operational worlds. While new to formal programme management, Dr Yu will be supported by UCL CCTU's dedicated trial coordinator and team, ensuring operational excellence. This structure allows her to focus on scientific leadership while developing PM expertise through mentored practice. As Programme Manager reporting to the CI, Dr Yu will transform strategic vision into executable plans. She will coordinate across five workstreams and multiple sites, managing day-to-day delivery and budget oversight. Her responsibilities span co-production workshops (WS1), field-testing logistics (WS2), trial operations partnership with CCTU (WS3), and implementation outputs (WS4-5). Dr Yu's scientific expertise in hearing-cognition pathways ensures clinical relevance drives operational decisions. Her proven ability to build consensus among diverse stakeholders provides the relationship skills essential for multi-site coordination.

Prof Anne Schilder is a Consultant ENT surgeon at UCLH and trialist, NIHR Senior Investigator and Director of the NIHR UCLH BRC Hearing Health Theme, where she uses qualitative and quantitative research methods to establish how best to educate ear and hearing care professionals about the role of cognition in hearing care for older adults, how to detect early cognitive impairment in ear and hearing clinics for older adults and provide tailored care to those identified with early cognitive impairment (Omar et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2025). As the past NIHR CRN ENT National Specialty Lead she has a large network across specialties involved in the proposed TACT+ programme and in- depth knowledge of research delivery within the NHS setting. She will provide senior clinical academic guidance throughout the programme and work with ENT and Audiology Specialties to ensure the trial intervention is robust and practically implementable within the NHS. Her experience in establishing best practice in hearing care will be vital in navigating the novel governance and clinical safety challenges of implementing a supported self-fitting pathway in a non- specialist primary care setting. She will oversee and actively participate in the co-production of the TACT+ pathway and its field testing (WS1, WS2). She will provide expert input in the definitive clinical trial protocol and advise on trial set- up and delivery (WS3), as well as the development of the implementation toolkit and dissemination strategy (WS4, WS5)
