WS5 - Dissemination: Linda and Terry will co-develop accessible outputs including Easy Read
summaries and subtitled videos, plus lead public engagement through meetings and webinars and
community networks.
For detailed PPIE activities by WS, see the Detailed Research Plan sections on WS1-5.
Governance and Support
PPI co-investigators hold full membership of the Programme Management Group. They will be given
comprehensive support for their role including training, equipment loans, and flexible meeting
arrangements. All contributions are fairly remunerated according to NIHR payment guidance.
Measuring Impact
We will monitor and reflect on impact through GRIPP2 logs and review involvement activity at each
milestone to ensure it is meaningful and valuable.
Investment in Partnership
Our £30,331 PPIE budget reflects genuine commitment: £15,160 for contributor payments and access
costs (based on NIHR’s 2025 rates), plus £15,171 for the PPIE lead's coordination and engagement
support.

sub_criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
sub_criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

sub_criteria.pros: The application explicitly targets high-deprivation areas (IMD 1-4) and ethnic minority communities. Specific strategies include recruitment via GP searches in deprived areas, provision of 4G tablets to reduce digital exclusion, and co-produced materials for cultural accessibility. Barriers like digital exclusion are actively mitigated with budgeted resources.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the strategies are specific, targeted, and well-resourced.
sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the strategies are specific, targeted, and well-resourced.

beyond cognition, how to approach coaching, and app simplification. Two public contributors became

8. Plain English summary was excellent
Thank you, we have updated it.
9. Capacity building
We removed the MSc post and now support two PhDs (behaviour change, audiology) and two pre-
doctoral RAs (audiology, implementation). Trial staff, including the research audiologist, will receive
protected time, mentorship, and training. This aligns with PGfAR and NIHR Academy guidance and
represents 19% of the budget. Prof Livingston is the Named Training Contact. See pp. 26–27.
10. Timeline and regulatory risks
We revised the timeline for realism, in consultation with our CTU: reduced total duration, but lengthened
the preparatory phase for hiring and approvals. We do not anticipate needing MHRA approval as all
devices used will hold CE/UKCA marks

Aims
Co-develop, evaluate and enable national roll out of TACT+ to preserve cognition, reduce inequalities
and improve quality of life for people with MCI and hearing loss.
Objectives
1. Co-design the TACT+ intervention, coaching, app and training (WS1).
2. Test feasibility in two PCN hubs (WS2).
3. Establish clinical and economic effectiveness in a large-scale RCT (WS3).
4. Conduct a mixed methods implementation study and NHS-ready toolkit (WS4).
5. Dissemination of results to influence policy, practice and public uptake, focusing on underserved
groups (WS5).
Methods
WS1 Co design (M1–12): co- workshops apply the Behaviour Change Wheel to co-create Tympa
assessment, immediate self-fit of hearing aids, app coaching and NHS audiology links.
WS2 Field Testing (M7–18): Two contrasting hubs (London, Manchester) enrol 62 participants to test
recruitment, workflows, feasibility and equity strategies.
WS3 RCT (M16–63): Two arm RCT with masked assessors (n = 424, 12 sites), including a 6-month
internal pilot. Eligibility: 55 y, MoCA 18–25 (screening rather than formal MCI diagnosis), symmetrical
30–60 dB loss. Intervention: TAC

ls, hearing loops, and live captioning as standard
• Financial support: we will fund costs of travel, caring needs, technology, training, and other
participation costs as required
• Tailored engagement: Members can contribute in ways that suit their preferences through in face to
face or remote meetings, written or oral contributions.
We will track our reach using NIHR Research Inclusion tools to ensure we are hearing from under-
represented voices.
Co-Production Across All WS
WS1 - Design: PPIE partners will co-create the intervention with the rest of the research team through
stakeholders workshops. Linda and Terry will lead accessibility reviews of all materials. We will hold
sessions with people from high-deprivation areas and ethnic minority background.
WS 2 & 3 - Testing and Trial: Panel members will help shape and advise during field testing and trial,
providing real-time feedback on critical trial issues such as recruitment and retention. They'll shape
interview guides, and co-author plain English summaries of findings.
WS4 - Implementation: PPIE partners will input into our implementation toolkit and test results in real
NHS settings, ensuring it works for diverse users an

d contexts.
WS5 - Dissemination: Linda and Terry will co-develop accessible outputs including Easy Read
summaries and subtitled videos, plus lead public engagement through meetings and webinars and
community networks.
For detailed PPIE activities by WS, see the Detailed Research Plan sections on WS1-5.
Governance and Support
PPI co-investigators hold full membership of the Programme Management Group. They will be given
comprehensive support for their role including training, equipment loans, and flexible meeting
arrangements. All contributions are fairly remunerated according to NIHR payment guidance.
Measuring Impact
We will monitor and reflect on impact through GRIPP2 logs and review involvement activity at each
milestone to ensure it is meaningful and valuable.
Investment in Partnership
Our £30,331 PPIE budget reflects genuine commitment: £15,160 for contributor payments and access
costs (based on NIHR’s 2025 rates), plus £15,171 for the PPIE lead's coordination and engagement
support.

sub_criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the inclusion plan is fully costed and aligned with the activities described.

A dedicated PPIE panel and co-production group are established for all workstreams.

Aims
Co-develop, evaluate and enable national roll out of TACT+ to preserve cognition, reduce inequalities
and improve quality of life for people with MCI and hearing loss.
Objectives
1. Co-design the TACT+ intervention, coaching, app and training (WS1).
2. Test feasibility in two PCN hubs (WS2).
3. Establish clinical and economic effectiveness in a large-scale RCT (WS3).
4. Conduct a mixed methods implementation study and NHS-ready toolkit (WS4).
5. Dissemination of results to influence policy, practice and public uptake, focusing on underserved
groups (WS5).
Methods
WS1 Co design (M1–12): co- workshops apply the Behaviour Change Wheel to co-create Tympa
assessment, immediate self-fit of hearing aids, app coaching and NHS audiology links.
WS2 Field Testing (M7–18): Two contrasting hubs (London, Manchester) enrol 62 participants to test
recruitment, workflows, feasibility and equity strategies.
WS3 RCT (M16–63): Two arm RCT with masked assessors (n = 424, 12 sites), including a 6-month
internal pilot. Eligibility: 55 y, MoCA 18–25 (screening rather than formal MCI diagnosis), symmetrical

selection, how to sensitively communicate assessment results to participants, benefits of hearing aids
beyond cognition, how to approach coaching, and app simplification. Two public contributors became
co-applicants and will continue to guide design, delivery, and governance. A PPIE co-investigator leads
involvement; a co-production group will support implementation and materials. Support and evaluation
structures are in place. See pp. 6–7, 24–25.
8. Plain English summary was excellent
Thank you, we have updated it.
9. Capacity building
We removed the MSc post and now support two PhDs (behaviour change, audiology) and two pre-
doctoral RAs (audiology, implementation). Trial staff, including the research audiologist, will receive
protected time, mentorship, and training. This aligns with PGfAR and NIHR Academy guidance and
represents 19% of the budget. Prof Livingston is the Named Training Contact. See pp. 26–27.
10. Timeline and regulatory risks
We revised the timeline for realism, in consultation with our CTU: reduced total duration, but lengthened
the preparatory phase for hiring and approvals. We do not anticipate needing MHRA approval as all
devices used will hold CE/UKCA marks

evidence.text:
Leadership Through Lived Experience
Our public involvement is co-led by Linda Kirby and Terry Emery, who bring complementary
perspectives on hearing and cognition as co-applicants. Linda, a retired university library assistant
diagnosed with congenital hearing loss in mid-life, combines professional expertise in clear
communication with personal insight into late-diagnosis challenges. Terry, a former copywriter who
contributed to the James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership on hearing and dementia, brings his
perspective on how hearing loss intersects with cognitive concerns.
Supporting them is Fahima Begum, our PPIE lead with over a decade of experience facilitating inclusive
research. She brings professional expertise and personal experience of caring for people with cognitive
impairment and hearing loss. This PPIE team works closely with Hannah Semeraro, RNID Head of
Insight and Evidence and audiologist.
Building an Inclusive Partnership
We will proactively recruit a diverse PPIE panel. Our commitment to inclusion means:
• Flexible participation: Hybrid meetings, with options for in-person, video, or phone involvement.
• Accessible formats: Large print, Easy Read materia

ls, hearing loops, and live captioning as standard
• Financial support: we will fund costs of travel, caring needs, technology, training, and other
participation costs as required
• Tailored engagement: Members can contribute in ways that suit their preferences through in face to

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Full Name:
Professor Sergi Costafreda Gonzalez

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Organisation:
University College London

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Department:
Mental Health of Older People

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Proposed Role:
I led the TACT pilot that proved supported hearing aids work for people with cognitive impairment: 75% uptake versus 22% in standard audiological care. I am positioned to adapt this innovation for primary care delivery and test its cognitive impact at scale through TACT+. As Professor of Psychiatry of Ageing at UCL and consultant at Islington Memory Service, I bridge research and clinical reality. I've spent a decade treating MCI while building research partnerships with GP practices. I currently direct ENHANCE (£2.9m NIHR programme), successfully recruiting healthy older adults through primary care networks—demonstrating my ability to engage GPs and navigate primary care pathways directly relevant to TACT+. Through ENHANCE, I've learned to design interventions that work when delivered by non-specialists, create training that builds confidence in primary care teams, and develop digital tools that work for older people and support busy practices. My £8m research portfolio spans sensory health, dementia prevention, technology and implementation science, generating 27,000 citations and shaping international policy through the Lancet Dementia Commission. I will reduce clinical sessions from 3 to 2 weekly to provide hands-on leadership across all five workstreams. My co-investigators bring multidisciplinary expertise in hearing research, primary care, implementation and trial operational excellence. TACT+ represents the convergence of my clinical expertise in cognitive ageing, proven ability to deliver complex interventions through primary care, and vision for accessible dementia prevention. I have the experience, relationships, and team to make this work in the real NHS.

Dr Ruan-Ching Yu

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [0] > Proposed Role:
Dr Ruan-Ching Yu brings unique qualifications to lead TACT+ programme management. As first author on the TACT pilot trial publication (Yu et al., 2025) and of the systematic review establishing hearing-dementia links (Yu et al., 2024), and with a PhD on cultural adaptation and validation of cognitive testing for MCI, she possesses deep knowledge of both the intervention and its evidence base. Her coordination of TACT pilot and this complex NIHR application shows her organisational capability. She has assembled a multidisciplinary research team, worked with PPI, navigated NHS governance structures, engaged Primary Care and RDNs, and built enthusiasm across diverse stakeholders. This achievement reveals her ability to translate between clinical, academic, and operational worlds. While new to formal programme management, Dr Yu will be supported by UCL CCTU's dedicated trial coordinator and team, ensuring operational excellence. This structure allows her to focus on scientific leadership while developing PM expertise through mentored practice. As Programme Manager reporting to the CI, Dr Yu will transform strategic vision into executable plans. She will coordinate across five workstreams and multiple sites, managing day-to-day delivery and budget oversight. Her responsibilities span co-production workshops (WS1), field-testing logistics (WS2), trial operations partnership with CCTU (WS3), and implementation outputs (WS4-5). Dr Yu's scientific expertise in hearing-cognition pathways ensures clinical relevance drives operational decisions. Her proven ability to build consensus among diverse stakeholders provides the relationship skills essential for multi-site coordination.

Professor Anne GM Schilder

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [1] > Organisation:
University College London

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [1] > Department:
Ear Institute

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [1] > Proposed Role:
Prof Anne Schilder is a Consultant ENT surgeon at UCLH and trialist, NIHR Senior Investigator and Director of the NIHR UCLH BRC Hearing Health Theme, where she uses qualitative and quantitative research methods to establish how best to educate ear and hearing care professionals about the role of cognition in hearing care for older adults, how to detect early cognitive impairment in ear and hearing clinics for older adults and provide tailored care to those identified with early cognitive impairment (Omar et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2025). As the past NIHR CRN ENT National Specialty Lead she has a large network across specialties involved in the proposed TACT+ programme and in- depth knowledge of research delivery within the NHS setting. She will provide senior clinical academic guidance throughout the programme and work with ENT and Audiology Specialties to ensure the trial intervention is robust and practically implementable within the NHS. Her experience in establishing best practice in hearing care will be vital in navigating the novel governance and clinical safety challenges of implementing a supported self-fitting pathway in a non- specialist primary care setting. She will oversee and actively participate in the co-production of the TACT+ pathway and its field testing (WS1, WS2). She will provide expert input in the definitive clinical trial protocol and advise on trial set- up and delivery (WS3), as well as the development of the implementation toolkit and dissemination strategy (WS4, WS5)

Dr Ross Dunne

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [2] > Department:
Later Life Psychiatry

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [2] > Proposed Role:
Ross Dunne is a Consultant Psychiatrist and Director of the Greater Manchester Dementia Research Centre. He led development of the "Mild Cognitive Impairment: the Manchester consensus" (Dunne et al., 2021), which has transformed clinical practice across the UK. This highly-cited publication provided the first UK expert consensus on diagnosing and managing MCI, standardising practice where formal guidance was lacking. Within TACT+, Dr Dunne will provide senior clinical oversight for cognitive eligibility criteria and lead the Manchester field-testing site. He will establish primary care partnerships and operational infrastructure for WS2 delivery, leveraging his local relationships to ensure efficient site set-up. His focus on equitable recruitment will help engage diverse Greater Manchester communities while ensuring trial governance meets NHS standards across mental health and primary care settings. He will support co-production of the TACT+ pathway, developing clinically grounded inclusion criteria (WS1), lead Manchester field- testing operations (WS2), co-develop the trial protocol for identifying potential MCI cases in primary care (WS3), and contribute to interpretation and dissemination of findings (WS5). His MCI expertise and established Manchester partnerships ensure TACT+ can rapidly mobilise in the region while reaching communities often underrepresented in research.

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [3] > Proposed Role:
Dr Helen Henshaw is a Cognitive Psychologist and Nottingham BRC Principal Research Fellow. She leads a programme of research in auditory rehabilitation and cognitive training, with expertise in promoting hearing aid use. As Chair of the British Society of Audiology's Cognition in Hearing Special Interest Group, she bridges hearing and cognitive sciences. She recently led the NIHR James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership for Dementia and Hearing Conditions, bringing patients, carers, and clinicians together to identify the field's top research priorities. Dr Henshaw will apply her behavioural science expertise to design the TACT+ support package, ensuring it maximises engagement and adherence among people with cognitive impairment. She will lead development of the hearing coach manual and hearing rehabilitation components of the TACT+ app during co-production (WS1). She will co- lead the optimisation and field-testing phase (WS2) and lead evaluation of intervention components within the process evaluation (WS4). Her unique combination of cognitive psychology, hearing rehabilitation expertise, and patient priority insights ensures TACT+ interventions are both evidence-based and aligned with user needs.

Fahima Khatun is PPIE and EDI Lead of the NIHR UCLH BRC Hearing Health Theme, where she leads a diverse panel of public contributors with lived experience of hearing conditions. As Director of a home care company in East London, she has extensive first-hand expertise supporting older adults with cognitive impairment and working with underserved communities across healthcare settings. This dual perspective gives her unique insight into the health inequalities and access barriers TACT+ aims to address. Ms Khatun will lead the programme's integrated PPIE and EDI strategy. She will ensure meaningful involvement is embedded throughout, grounded in established frameworks (UK Standards for Public Involvement, 4Pi). She will co-design and facilitate inclusive co-production workshops (WS1), develop the EDI strategy to support recruitment from underserved communities (WS3), co-chair the PPIE panel to shape research throughout (WS1-5), and ensure dissemination outputs are co-produced and accessible for people with hearing loss and cognitive difficulties (WS5). Her unique combination of academic PPIE leadership and daily experience caring for older adults with cognitive impairment ensures TACT+ remains grounded in real-world needs while advancing equity in dementia prevention research.

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [5] > Proposed Role:
Dr Liz Steed is Reader in Health Psychology and an expert in behaviour change and implementation science. She has extensive experience developing theory-based interventions for successful trials (STAMINA, RP-PG-1016-2007) and leading process evaluations in implementation trials (IMP2ART RRP-PG-1016-20008, PETAL). As implementation lead for ENHANCE, she has established strong working relationships with the current team. Internationally, she teaches behaviour change in pulmonary rehabilitation and is an active member of the European Respiratory Society's Psychology and Behavioural Scientists division. Dr Steed will lead TACT+'s process evaluation and implementation strategy, ensuring the pathway is grounded in behavioural and implementation theory while generating deep understanding of how it can be adopted and sustained across diverse NHS primary care contexts. She will co-lead co-production using the COM-B model and Theoretical Domains Framework (WS1). Her leadership will be central to WS4, where she will conduct mixed-methods process evaluation using Normalisation Process Theory and develop the theory-informed implementation toolkit for NHS adoption. Her expertise ensures TACT+ moves beyond efficacy to real-world implementation from the outset.

She will provide senior operational oversight for the full-size multi-site RCT (WS3), ensuring delivery to the highest methodological and regulatory standards. Her expertise in troubleshooting common trial delays will be invaluable in mitigating risks and ensuring efficient delivery of high- quality results on time and budget. Ms Ikeji will oversee the CCTU team responsible for day-to-day trial management, including protocol development, ethics and regulatory approvals, site set-up, and implementation of robust recruitment and data management strategies. She will ensure that all trial operations maintain GCP compliance throughout. Her leadership ensures TACT+ benefits from UCL CCTU's established infrastructure and proven track record in operational excellence in delivering complex multi-site trials.

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [7] > Proposed Role:
Dr Devina Maru is a practising GP with ENT special interest, Co-Deputy Director for Medical Education in Primary Care at UCL, and locum GP at Islington iHUB Enhanced Access Service. She shaped the RCGP Hearing Loss Education Toolkit, led a £169K Regulators Pioneers Fund project on health inequalities, and advised the NIHR JLA Priority Setting Partnership for Dementia and Hearing Conditions. As former RCGP National Clinical Champion for Hearing Loss and current Primary Care representative on the NIHR UCLH BRC Hearing Health Theme Board, she brings deep experience converting national policy into practical, equitable primary care services. In WS1, Dr Maru will steer co-production of the hearing pathway to dovetail with GP workflows, digital priorities and ICB transformation agendas, advising on EMIS/SystmOne search protocols and practice- nurse scripts. During WS2 field-testing, she will address usability or equity barriers emerging in practices. In WS3, she will advise on clinician and patient engagement strategies for inclusive recruitment across diverse communities. In WS4, she will co-lead development of the implementation toolkit and CPD resources, ensuring the hearing- coach model is ready for scalable primary care adoption. Her dual perspective as frontline GP and policy leader ensures TACT+ remains grounded in primary care reality while advancing system-wide change.

Comprehensive Clinical Trial Unit

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [8] > Proposed Role:
Monica Panca is UCL CCTU Senior Health Economist with expertise in economic evaluation within clinical trials. She has extensive experience conducting economic evaluations (within-trial and decision modelling) across different disease areas, including cognitive impairment. Ms Panca will lead the economic evaluation of TACT+, providing robust evidence on value for money and affordability to inform NHS commissioners and policymakers, supporting real-world uptake if the intervention proves effective. Within the main trial, she will oversee development of relevant CRFs, protocol and health economic analysis plan. She will conduct cost- effectiveness analyses from NHS/Personal Social Services and societal perspectives, using trial data on resource use, intervention costs, and quality of life. She will also lead long-term modelling to project NHS implications beyond the trial timeframe and budget impact analysis for national rollout (WS3). Her expertise ensures TACT+ generates the economic evidence essential for commissioning decisions and implementation planning.

Dr Hannah Semeraro is an Audiologist and Head of Insight and Evidence at RNID, the national charity supporting people who are deaf, have hearing loss, or tinnitus. Her PhD focused on developing novel tools to assess functional hearing ability, and her clinical and teaching experience includes training clinical audiologists. As Head of Insight and Evidence at RNID, Dr Semeraro brings subject- matter expertise and connects TACT+ to the communities RNID supports. She will ensure the intervention is patient-centred, equitable, and informed by lived experience. RNID's extensive networks and insights into barriers faced by people with hearing loss will strengthen the design, delivery, and dissemination of TACT+. Dr Semeraro will support co-production of the TACT+ pathway and co- lead development of the training package for primary care staff, drawing on her audiology teaching experience (WS1). She will co-lead public and third-sector engagement activities, ensuring outputs are accessible and inclusive (WS5). RNID will publicise the trial through its communication channels, support recruitment via its networks, and help disseminate findings. RNID's policymaker relationships will support translation of TACT+ findings into national policy recommendations (WS5). Her dual role as clinical expert and charity leader ensures TACT+ bridges research excellence with real-world impact for people with hearing loss.

Audiology

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [10] > Proposed Role:
Gareth Smith is Clinician Scientist in Audiology, Head of Audiological Science and Clinical Lead at Mid and South Essex NHS Foundation Trust. As former President of the British Academy of Audiology and current Audiology Editor for ENT & Audiology News, his expertise lies in strategic conception and operational delivery of advanced clinical audiology services within the NHS. In TACT+, Mr Smith will lead technical and clinical development of the hearing intervention. Crucially, he will guide selection of appropriate self-fitting hearing aids for our MCI population, drawing on his deep knowledge of the rapidly evolving OTC market and clinical evidence base. His frontline NHS experience ensures we choose devices that balance innovation with practical usability for cognitively vulnerable users. He will co-create protocols converting Tympa diagnostic data into safe fitting parameters and remote validation dashboards operable by primary care staff, ensuring the pathway is clinically sound and implementable within NHS realities. He will co-lead development and operationalisation of the pathway (WS1-2) and provide expert guidance on NHS device procurement and data tracking (WS4). His clinical acumen and finger on the pulse of hearing technology ensures TACT+ selects and implements solutions that work in real- world practice.

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [11] > Proposed Role:
Prof Nick Freemantle is UCL CCTU Director, Professor of Clinical Epidemiology & Biostatistics, and a leading trial methodologist. He will provide senior oversight for the programme's entire methodological design, ensuring the highest standards of rigour. As ultimate guarantor of the trial's robustness and credibility, he will ensure the design directly addresses funding committee feedback. His leadership will focus on the definitive RCT (WS3). He will direct the statistical analysis plan, ensuring alignment with CONSORT-Equity extension to investigate effects in diverse subgroups, and provide expert guidance to the independent oversight committees (TSC/DMEC). Prof Freemantle's involvement ensures TACT+ meets the gold standard for clinical trial conduct and produces robust evidence, fit for NHS decision-making and change of practice.

Prof Gill Livingston is a Consultant Psychiatrist and world-leading expert in dementia prevention, serving as lead author of the Lancet Dementia Commission. An experienced trialist in cognitive impairment and dementia, she brings deep expertise in participant recruitment, retention, and carer engagement. She will provide senior clinical and academic guidance throughout TACT+, advising on strategies to ensure patient acceptability and trial success. As named lead for Research Capacity Building, she will oversee development of our early career researchers. Her expertise will be key for interpreting trial findings in the context of national and international dementia strategy. Her proven dissemination skills, evidenced by the global impact of the Lancet Commission, will help translate TACT+ findings into policy and practice change (WS5). She will oversee two PhD studentships and two research associate posts, ensuring the programme builds lasting capacity in hearing and dementia research. Prof Livingston's leadership ensures TACT+ delivers both rigorous evidence and real-world impact in dementia prevention.

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [13] > Proposed Role:
Prof Nishchay Mehta is Consultant ENT surgeon at UCLH, Chair of Otology and Auditory Implants, AI Scientist and Co-director of the NIHR UCLH BRC Hearing Health Theme. He leads data-driven research applying AI to routine NHS data to understand hearing loss phenotypes and develop targeted treatments. He co-leads the NIHR I4I grant developing soft robotics for inner ear therapeutics. Critically for TACT+, he led development and rollout of remote ear and hearing care pathways in North London PCNs using Tympa technology, and worked with NHSE to expand this nationally to 350+ community pharmacies. This same Tympa audiometry technology will guide self-fitting of hearing aids in TACT+. Together with co-applicants representing Audiology, Dementia, and Primary Care, he will co-lead design and operationalisation of the TACT+ pathway (WS1-2), advise on pragmatic workflows, training and governance of hearing coaches (WS2-3), and guide integration of the TACT+ app into NHS pathways (WS4-5). His unique combination of clinical expertise, NHS systems knowledge, and proven ability to scale digital hearing care nationally positions him to ensure TACT+ delivers practical, implementable solutions.

Mr Terry Emery

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [14] > Organisation:

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [14] > Department:
Hearing Health theme

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [14] > Proposed Role:
Terry Emery is a former freelance copywriter and public representative within the NIHR UCLH BRC Hearing Health Theme PPIE panel, bringing personal lived experience of age-related hearing loss to research. He has contributed to major initiatives including the James Lind Alliance Priority Setting Partnership, which identified the top 10 research priorities for mild-to-moderate hearing loss, and serves as lay member of the DHSC/NHS England working group that co-produced the national Hearing Loss & Tinnitus Research Action Plan, translating policy into practice. As public co-applicant on TACT+, Mr Emery will sit on the Programme Management Group and Trial Steering Committee, ensuring lived- experience insights shape governance decisions, recruitment strategy and participant retention. Crucially, he will guide co-development of the intervention and its digital components, advising how to make them accessible for people with memory or hearing difficulties. He will co-produce the TACT+ pathway and participant-facing materials, ensuring plain-language clarity (WS1). Together with co-applicant Linda Kirby, he will co-lead the lay dissemination plan—developing plain- English summaries, identifying community channels and creating accessible formats—ensuring findings reach the broadest public audience (WS5). His professional communication skills combined with personal hearing loss experience uniquely position him to bridge research and public understanding.

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Co-Applicants > [15] > Proposed Role:
Linda Kirby is a retired university library assistant and long-standing public representative within the NIHR UCLH BRC Hearing Health Theme PPIE panel. Diagnosed with congenital hearing loss in mid-life, she combines lived experience with professional expertise in plain- language communication and inclusive resource curation, skills that align directly with TACT+'s aims to create accessible, patient-centred information. As public co-applicant on TACT+, Ms Kirby will hold a seat on the Programme Management Group and Trial Steering Committee, providing ongoing scrutiny of recruitment and retention plans. Alongside Terry Emery, she will co-design the pathway and digital interface, advising on layout, navigation and multimedia cues that work for people with hearing and memory challenges. She will conduct detailed readability and usability reviews of study documents and app screens (WS1), and co-lead the lay dissemination strategy to ensure results are shared in meaningful, accessible ways (WS5). Her unique combination of information science expertise and personal hearing loss experience ensures TACT+ materials truly serve those who need them most.

criteria.name: Commitment to Maximising Public and Patient Benefit
criteria.definition: Clarity and strength of the plan to ensure research outcomes meaningfully benefit patients, carers, and the public.

signals.signal_text: Mentions of dissemination, knowledge translation, or implementation plans
Clear links between study objectives and anticipated benefits
Emphasis on actionable outcomes or improvements in health/care

criteria.pros: Dissemination plans are robust and co-produced, including lay summaries, policy briefs, and accessible formats (Easy Read, subtitled videos). Clear links are made between study objectives (preserving cognition) and anticipated benefits (reducing inequalities, NHS rollout). Actionable outcomes include an NHS-ready toolkit and commissioning briefs.
criteria.drawbacks: No significant drawbacks; the plan is specific, actionable, and directly aligned with patient and public benefit.
criteria.rationale: No significant drawbacks; the plan is specific, actionable, and directly aligned with patient and public benefit.

evidence.text:
T+; Care as usual: audiometry feedback with advice to consult GP.
Primary outcome: MoCA change at 18 months; secondary outcomes: cost-effectiveness, dementia
incidence, hearing outcomes, mood, social function.
WS4 Implementation (M1–63): Normalisation Process Theory guided evaluation with fidelity metrics and
>50 stakeholder interviews to identify barriers, enablers and core-plus-adaptable elements; outputs
include commissioning briefs, training resources and budget tools.
WS5 Dissemination (M36–63): Co created lay summaries, policy briefs, CPD resources and open-
source code; partnerships with RNID, NIHR Evidence and third sector groups maximise reach into
deprived and ethnic minority communities.
Impact
If effective, TACT+ will offer a low cost, digitally enabled route to preserve cognition, halve progression
to dementia, and reduce inequalities by reaching high deprivation PCNs. Outputs - open access toolkit,
commissioning brief, and co-developed PPIE resources—will position the NHS to roll out hearing care
prevention at scale within six years.

od Health Centres and GP practices, and talk to patients and staff about their experiences.
What we learn will help us make it easier for the NHS to use this approach in the future.
People and Communities:
Our lived experience panel has shaped this project from the start and will continue to be involved. They
will co-design the intervention, help with study materials and recruitment and guide how we share results
and plan for future use in the NHS.
We are committed to including people from all backgrounds, especially those who are often left out of
research. Our team has a strong track record of working with diverse communities, so the findings can
be useful for everyone.
Sharing the Results:
We will share what we learn with the public, national charities, and patient groups — who will also help
us spread the word. Together, we’ll share updates through a website, social media, and newsletters,
and we’ll present at public events and publish in journals. If it works well, we’ll use what we’ve learned to
help the NHS offer this approach more widely.

criteria.name: Representation of Seldom Heard or Underserved Groups
criteria.definition: Degree to which participants and PPI contributors represent groups historically underrepresented in research, including those facing health inequalities.

signals.signal_text: Mention of specific demographic groups (age, race, ethnicity, disability, gender, sexual orientation, religion/belief, location, socioeconomic status)
Evidence of targeted recruitment strategies
Discussion of barriers to participation and mitigation strategies

criteria.pros: The application explicitly targets high-deprivation areas (IMD 1-4) and ethnic minority communities. Specific strategies include recruitment via GP searches in deprived areas, provision of 4G tablets to reduce digital exclusion, and co-produced materials for cultural accessibility. Barriers like digital exclusion are actively mitigated with budgeted resources.
criteria.drawbacks: No material caveats; the strategies are specific, targeted, and well-resourced.
criteria.rationale: No material caveats; the strategies are specific, targeted, and well-resourced.

8. Plain English summary was excellent
Thank you, we have updated it.
9. Capacity building
We removed the MSc post and now support two PhDs (behaviour change, audiology) and two pre-
doctoral RAs (audiology, implementation). Trial staff, including the research audiologist, will receive
protected time, mentorship, and training. This aligns with PGfAR and NIHR Academy guidance and
represents 19% of the budget. Prof Livingston is the Named Training Contact. See pp. 26–27.
10. Timeline and regulatory risks
We revised the timeline for realism, in consultation with our CTU: reduced total duration, but lengthened
the preparatory phase for hiring and approvals. We do not anticipate needing MHRA approval as all
devices used will hold CE/UKCA marks

Aims
Co-develop, evaluate and enable national roll out of TACT+ to preserve cognition, reduce inequalities
and improve quality of life for people with MCI and hearing loss.
Objectives
1. Co-design the TACT+ intervention, coaching, app and training (WS1).
2. Test feasibility in two PCN hubs (WS2).
3. Establish clinical and economic effectiveness in a large-scale RCT (WS3).
4. Conduct a mixed methods implementation study and NHS-ready toolkit (WS4).
5. Dissemination of results to influence policy, practice and public uptake, focusing on underserved
groups (WS5).
Methods
WS1 Co design (M1–12): co- workshops apply the Behaviour Change Wheel to co-create Tympa
assessment, immediate self-fit of hearing aids, app coaching and NHS audiology links.
WS2 Field Testing (M7–18): Two contrasting hubs (London, Manchester) enrol 62 participants to test
recruitment, workflows, feasibility and equity strategies.
WS3 RCT (M16–63): Two arm RCT with masked assessors (n = 424, 12 sites), including a 6-month
internal pilot. Eligibility: 55 y, MoCA 18–25 (screening rather than formal MCI diagnosis), symmetrical
30–60 dB loss. Intervention: TAC

ls, hearing loops, and live captioning as standard

sub_criteria.name: Use of Formatting, Headings, and Subheadings
sub_criteria.definition: Effective use of text formatting to make the application clear, navigable, and easy to read.

signals.signal_text: Presence and consistency of headings and subheadings
Use of bold or emphasis for key terms
Structured hierarchy of sections (logical nesting of headings)
Patterns detected via text parsing for formatting markers

sub_criteria.pros: The application form analysis confirms a robust structural hierarchy with 7 detected sections, 18 numbered heading-like lines, and 43 list markers, indicating consistent use of headings and subheadings. The presence of 128 extracted emphasis markers and 1 table-like budget line demonstrates effective use of formatting to highlight key terms and structure data.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: While emphasis markers are detected, the analysis notes a parser limitation where bold/emphasis may be lost during extraction; thus, the score for emphasis (af.1.b) is capped at 4 to account for the potential loss of visual formatting fidelity in the raw text, though the structural evidence remains strong.

sub_criteria.name: Limited Duplication and Repetition
sub_criteria.definition: Avoidance of unnecessary repetition within or across sections.

sub_criteria.name: Logical Flow and Coherence Across Sections
sub_criteria.definition: The application progresses logically, with a clear thread linking the research question, objectives, and work packages.

signals.signal_text: Semantic coherence between sections (using embeddings or similarity metrics)
Explicit linking of objectives to methods and work packages
Presence of transitional phrases or linking statements
Detectable narrative thread anchored on research aims

sub_criteria.pros: The application exhibits a logical section order moving from context to research plan, PPI, and budget. Strong evidence of coherence is provided by 45 objective-method-budget link terms, suggesting a tight narrative thread connecting aims to execution. The presence of 3 transition phrases supports logical flow.
sub_criteria.drawbacks: The count of transition phrases (3) is relatively low for a document of this size, suggesting that while the structural logic is sound, explicit transitional linking statements between sections may be sparse. Additionally, semantic coherence metrics (embeddings) are not explicitly provided in the analysis, relying instead on structural indicators.
sub_criteria.rationale: The count of transition phrases (3) is relatively low for a document of this size, suggesting that while the structural logic is sound, explicit transitional linking statements between sections may be sparse. Additionally, semantic coherence metrics (embeddings) are not explicitly provided in the analysis, relying instead on structural indicators.

findings.implication: Scientific Abstract: Clearly outlines problem, objectives, methods, and impact, supporting pr.1.
Scientific Abstract: Cites ACHIEVE trial and TACT pilot to justify the study, supporting pr.2.
Scientific Abstract: Defines 5 workstreams with specific timelines and sample sizes, supporting pr.3.
Scientific Abstract: Details RCT design, eligibility, and outcomes, supporting pr.4.
Scientific Abstract: Highlights potential to halve dementia progression and reduce inequalities, supporting pr.5.
Scientific Abstract: Mentions co-design workshops and PPIE resources, supporting pr.6.
Scientific Abstract: Focuses on deprived PCNs and ethnic minority communities, supporting pr.9.
Scientific Abstract: Describes co-design of intervention and app with stakeholders, supporting wp.1.
Scientific Abstract: Plans dissemination via lay summaries and policy briefs, supporting wp.3.
Scientific Abstract: Explicitly targets deprived and ethnic minority communities, supporting wp.4.
Plain English Summary of Research: Clearly explains the problem (MCI/hearing loss), objectives, methods (RCT with hearing aids/coach), and impact in accessible language, supporting pr.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Outlines a 5-year timeline with specific phases (setup, trial, analysis) and recruitment via GP practices, supporting pr.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Describes a comparative study design with two groups (intervention vs. usual care) and specific outcome measures (memory, mood, costs), supporting pr.4.
Plain English Summary of Research: Highlights benefits such as better hearing, mood, social life, and potential delay of dementia, supporting pr.5.
Plain English Summary of Research: Details a lived experience panel involved in co-design, recruitment, and dissemination, supporting pr.6.
Plain English Summary of Research: Explicitly commits to including people from all backgrounds and those often left out of research, supporting pr.9.
Plain English Summary of Research: States the lived experience panel will co-design the intervention and study materials, supporting wp.1.
Plain English Summary of Research: Plans to share results with public, charities, and patient groups via website, social media, and events, supporting wp.3.
Plain English Summary of Research: Commits to including people from all backgrounds, especially those often left out, supporting wp.4.
Changes from Previous Stage: Justifies revised timeline (66 months) and feasibility by shifting to primary care hubs and confirming CE/UKCA marks for devices, supporting pr.3.
Changes from Previous Stage: Details methodological shifts to MoCA, supported self-fitting, and 18-month follow-up with power calculations, supporting pr.4.
Changes from Previous Stage: Justifies £3.42M budget against expanded scope but notes exclusion of open access costs, partially supporting pr.7.
Changes from Previous Stage: Describes recruitment in high-deprivation areas (IMD 1-4), 4G tablets for digital exclusion, and co-produced materials, strongly supporting pr.9.
Changes from Previous Stage: Outlines support for two PhDs and two RAs with mentorship and training aligned to PGfAR/NIHR guidance, supporting td.1.
Changes from Previous Stage: Links training in behaviour change and audiology to specific project roles and implementation needs, supporting td.2.
Changes from Previous Stage: Highlights collaboration with RDNs and existing ENHANCE recruitment in GP practices, supporting ss.1.
Changes from Previous Stage: Mentions Prof Livingston as Named Training Contact and protected time for staff, supporting ss.2.
Changes from Previous Stage: Details PPIE influence on design, outcomes, and app simplification, with two public contributors as co-applicants, supporting wp.1.
Changes from Previous Stage: Explicitly targets high-deprivation areas and plans analysis by IMD, ethnicity, and age, supporting wp.4.
Patient & Public Involvement: Details co-creation of intervention in WS1, shaping interview guides in WS2/3, and co-developing outputs in WS5, strongly supporting wp.1.
Patient & Public Involvement: Describes a PPIE team with lived experience of hearing loss and cognitive impairment, supporting wp.2.
Patient & Public Involvement: Plans for co-developing accessible outputs (Easy Read, subtitled videos) and public engagement via webinars, supporting wp.3.
Patient & Public Involvement: Commits to recruiting diverse panels, targeting high-deprivation areas and ethnic minorities, and using NIHR tools to track under-represented voices, supporting wp.4.
Patient & Public Involvement: Outlines involvement across all workstreams (Design, Testing, Implementation, Dissemination) with specific roles, supporting wp.5.
Patient & Public Involvement: Provides a specific £30,331 budget breakdown for payments, access costs, and coordination, supporting wp.6.
Patient & Public Involvement: Details accessible formats (Easy Read, hearing loops, live captioning) and flexible participation options to ensure inclusivity, supporting pr.9.

SUMMARY BUDGET: Provides detailed cost breakdowns for personnel, digital infrastructure, and equipment, explicitly benchmarked against NIHR guidance and linked to deliverables, strongly supporting pr.7.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Allocates specific funds for 278 tablet devices and SIM cards for participants lacking digital access, aligned with NIHR inclusion guidance, supporting pr.9.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Includes a dedicated £30.3k line item for PPI fees covering participation, travel, and coordination, confirming a costed plan for inclusion, supporting wp.6.
SUMMARY BUDGET: Budgets for 2 PhD fees/stipends and includes Tympa Academy training for coaches, indicating structured training activities aligned with project needs, supporting td.1.

Application Context: Lead applicant details decade of MCI treatment, ENHANCE programme direction, and TACT pilot leadership; co-applicants list specific expertise in audiology, primary care, and implementation science, s
Application Context: Lead applicant cites 27,000 citations and Lancet Dementia Commission; co-applicants reference high-impact consensus statements and systematic reviews, supporting g.4.

Application Context: Lead applicant directs £2.9m NIHR programme and leads TACT+; co-applicants hold senior roles (NIHR Senior Investigator, Consultant Psychiatrist, Head of Clinical Trial Operations), demonstrating incr

Application Context: Lead applicant is a consultant psychiatrist; co-applicants include practising GPs, ENT surgeons, and audiologists actively delivering clinical care while leading research, strongly supporting g.6.
Application Context: Lead applicant cites successful TACT pilot (75% uptake) and ENHANCE recruitment; co-applicants reference prior successful trials and consensus statements, supporting pr.8.
Application Context: UCL CCTU provides operational oversight and infrastructure; partnerships with NHS trusts and RNID offer clinical pathways and dissemination networks, supporting ss.3.

Application Context: Dedicated PPIE and EDI lead (Ms Khatun) with lived experience; team includes public co-applicants (Mr Emery, Ms Kirby) and explicit focus on underserved communities, supporting ss.4.

Application Context: Team includes diverse expertise (psychology, audiology, primary care, health economics) and public representatives with lived experience of hearing loss and cognitive impairment, supporting wp.2.
Plain English NLP Analysis: Coverage hits confirm presence of objectives, methods, beneficiaries, and impact, supporting pr.1 content requirements.
